Australia.impact:
In general, Australia does not exclude foreign firms from public procurement. At the federal level, officials from corporate and non-corporate Commonwealth entities listed in the Public Governance, Performance, and Accountability (PGPA) Rule 2014 must comply with the Commonwealth Procurement Rules (CPRs) (CPRs 2.1-2.2). CPRs contain a non-discrimination principle, which provides that " All potential suppliers to government must, subject to these CPRs, be treated equitably based on their commercial, legal, technical and financial abilities and not be discriminated
against due to their size, degree of foreign affiliation or ownership, location, or the origin of
their goods and services." (CPRs 5.4).

However, the Australian Government retains the discretion to exclude foreign firms from participating in critical national infrastructure. This power has been exercised in the past for national security reasons, as seen with the 2012 ban on Huawei from the National Broadband Network (NBN) rollout. Similarly, in 2018, both Huawei and ZTE were banned from Australia’s 5G rollout on similar grounds. In 2023, TikTok was also banned from federal government devices due to security concerns under the Protective Security Policy Framework.
No requirements to surrender source codes or use certain encryption standards are found as a condition to win tenders.
Australia.timeframe: Since June 2024

Australia.impact:
There are no limitations in procurement bidding; however, the Australian Industry Participation (AIP) plan ensures that Australian industry is "given a full, fair, and reasonable opportunity to participate" in certain government-funded and major projects. The plan does not affect the commercial decisions of funding recipients or mandate the use of Australian industry.

The AIP plan applies to government grants, contracts, and payments of $20 million AUD or more, as well as to major projects valued at $500 million AUD or above. For projects subject to an AIP plan, procurement entities cannot request bids for key goods or services unless they have a broad understanding of Australian entities' capacity and capability to supply those goods or services.
Australia.timeframe: Since June 2013, last amended in October 2024

Australia.impact:
The Commonwealth Procurement Rules (CPRs) outline the Australian Government's commitment to ensuring that non-corporate Commonwealth entities procure at least 25% of contracts by value from small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) for contracts valued up to $1 billion (5.6). Additionally, the Government has set a target for these entities to procure 40% of contracts by value from SMEs for contracts valued up to $20 million (5.7). SMEs include Australian and New Zealand firms with fewer than 200 full-time equivalent employees.

Procurement must also evaluate the broader economic benefits for projects valued over $4 million for non-construction goods and services, and over $7.5 million for construction services. This includes assessing how the procurement can increase productivity, better utilise under-employed resources, and support the development of key industry capabilities.
Australia.timeframe: Since June 2024

Australia.impact: The Commonwealth Indigenous Procurement Policy (IPP) promotes Indigenous business participation through annual targets, the Mandatory Set Aside (MSA), and the Mandatory Minimum Indigenous Participation Requirements (MMR). The MSA requires Indigenous businesses to have the first opportunity to demonstrate value for money for contracts between $80,000 and $200,000, or in remote areas. MMR applies to contracts over $7.5 million in 19 specific industry sectors, mandating Indigenous involvement.
Australia.timeframe: Since March 2015, last amended December 2020

Australia.impact:
The Foreign Acquisitions and Takeovers Act 1975 (FATA), overseen by the Foreign Investment Review Board (FIRB), regulates foreign investments and acquisitions in Australia. FATA enables the government to screen, approve, or block foreign investments that pose national security risks. Typically, FIRB approval is required when a foreign investor seeks at least a 20% interest. However, this threshold does not apply to foreign government investors, their associates, or to businesses classified as "national security businesses," all of which must obtain FIRB approval.

The Security of Critical Infrastructure Act 2018 (SOCI Act) designates 11 sectors as "critical infrastructure assets" that require FIRB approval, including communications, financial services, data storage, and energy. Foreign investments/acquisitions of businesses in these sectors must undergo mandatory FIRB screening and receive FIRB approval after evaluation.

In June 2024, the Australian government ordered five international companies linked to China to divest their shares in a strategically crucial Australian heavy rare earths project, following advice from the Foreign Investment Review Board (FIRB).

Australia.impact:
No local presence requirement is found in the Patents Act 1990 as a prerequisite for applying for a patent. Although not limited to patents related to digital goods and processes, the World Intellectual Property Organization reports that in the past 10 years the number of foreign patent applicants and actual grants have been far greater than the domestic counterpart. 

Foreign patent applicants can apply for patent application through the Patent Cooperation Treaty system, allowing a singular patent to be applied to multiple countries.

Article 29B outlines special rules for Convention applications. These applications, filed under an international patent treaty, allow inventors to claim priority from an earlier patent application filed in another country. To file a Convention application, inventors must provide details of the earlier application and a complete specification.
Australia.timeframe: Since October 1990, last amended in October 2024

Australia.impact: Patent enforcement is conducted under the Patents Act 1990. Civil enforcement procedures (Section 117, 120, 122) are primarily handled by the Federal Court of Australia, which has jurisdiction over patent disputes, including infringement cases and provisional measures (Section 123). Administrative matters related to patents, such as examinations and grants, are overseen by IP Australia, the national intellectual property agency.

Australia.impact:
Under Australian law, a 'trade secret' is considered to be merely a form of confidential information and does not have a clear or rigid meaning.

While Australia does not have a specific statutory regime for protecting trade secrets, there are various Acts which protect against the disclosure of confidential information through imposing an obligation of confidence. 

The Corporations Act 2001 provides that a person who obtains information because they are, or have been, a director or other officer or employee of a corporation must not improperly use the information.

Similarly, the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 states that a bargain between two parties never requires disclosure of a trade secret. 
In addition to claims for breach of contractual and statutory obligations of confidence, a common method of enforcing trade secrets that have been unlawfully disclosed is through the bringing of an equitable action for breach of confidence.

In addition, Australia complies with its obligations under article 39 of the 1995 Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights Agreement (TRIPS) by giving trade secrets legal protection through the equitable doctrine of "breach of confidence" and, where an appropriate clause exists, breach of contract.

Telecommunications carriers have statutory rights of access to other carriers' towers and ducts, but such access is not mandated. Passive infrastructure sharing is practiced in Australia in the mobile sector: co-location is negotiated on a commercial basis. Carriers have regulated rights of access to towers owned by other carriers for the purposes of providing carriage services. The Telecommunications Act 1997 provides for carriers to provide other carriers with access to telecommunications transmission towers, the sites of telecommunications transmission towers and eligible underground facilities. The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has made a code which sets out conditions that are to be complied with in relation to the provision of access.

Passive infrastructure sharing is also practiced in Australia in the fixed sector: the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has declared the following services: - line sharing service (LSS) - local carriage service (LCS) - fixed originating access service (FOAS) - fixed terminating access service (FTAS) - unconditioned local loop service (ULLS) - wholesale line rental (WLR) - wholesale ADSL service (WADSL) Once a service is declared, a network owner must provide access to the service upon request.
Australia.timeframe: Since April 1997, last amended in October 2024

Australia.impact:
The Telecommunications Act 1997 provides that "[a] condition of a carrier license may relate to the extent of foreign ownership or control (whether direct or indirect) of the holder" (Section 65). This restriction has been in place since its enactment in 1997. The Telstra Corporations Act 1991 provides under Section 8BG aggregate foreign ownership of Telstra (the incumbent telecommunication company) is limited to 35% and individual foreign investors are only allowed to own up to 5%. This specific cap was imposed in 1999 as part of an amendment of the Act. 

Note that Telstra Corporation was once a government-owned telecommunications provider. The Telstra Corporations Act established to manage the transition to a privatized company.

Australia.impact: The Privacy Act 1988 is the primary federal legislation that governs protection of personal information and applies horizontally. The Act includes thirteen Privacy Principles, which set out standards, rights and obligations for the handling, holding, use, accessing and correction of personal information (including sensitive information. The Privacy Act's amendment in 2018 introduced a mandatory notification scheme for entities in responding to data breaches. The Notifiable Data Breach Scheme (NDB scheme) introduced an obligation to notify individuals whose personal information is involved in a data breach that is likely to result in serious harm. The NDB scheme applies to all agencies and organisations with existing personal information security obligations under the Australian Privacy Act. The Privacy Act's amendment in 2023 increases the maximum penalties for serious or repeated privacy breaches and provides the Australian Information Commissioner with greater powers to resolve privacy breaches and quickly share information about data breaches.

Australia.impact:
Australia's Cyber Security is guided through the 2023-2030 Australian Cyber Security Strategy, which includes a roadmap for new legislation and standards. 

Until the new legislation under the strategy is passed, Cybersecurity is controlled through a mixture of laws including the Security of Critical Infrastructure Act 2018, Privacy Act 1988 and the Criminal Code Act 1995. These laws contain cybersecurity-related provisions dispersed across sectoral and thematic statutes, addressing critical infrastructure protection, data security, and cybercrime respectively, but they do not constitute a single, coherent, and horizontally applicable cybersecurity-specific legal framework.

The Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979 requires a telecommunication service provider to keep specific telecommunications data relating to the services it offers for two years (187C). The dataset to be kept includes: the subscriber and the accounts of telecommunications devices, the source of a communication, the destination of a communication, the date, time and duration of a communication, the type of a communication, and the location of equipment or a line used (187AA). This retention scheme was inserted into the Act by the Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Amendment (Data Retention) Act 2015.
Australia.timeframe: Since October 1979, last amended in October 2024

Australia.impact: A Privacy Impact Assessment ('PIA') is contemplated by Australian privacy law but, apart from government agencies, is not mandated. However, arguably a PIA is, if not required, highly recommended to fulfil one's obligations under APP 1.2. The guidance and recommendations of the OAIC are that a PIA should be used for any new, changed/varied or altered process, method, or technology used that processes any personal information. In addition, the OAIC may, in writing, direct an agency to conduct a PIA, within a specified period if an agency proposes to engage in an activity or function involving the handling of personal information about individuals, and the OAIC considers that the activity or function might have a significant impact on the privacy of individual (Section 33D(1) of the Privacy Act).
Australia.timeframe: Since 2020

Australia.impact: On 1 April 2022, the Data Availability and Transparency Act 2022 commenced, creating a new scheme for sharing Commonwealth Government data. Under the Act, authorised Commonwealth bodies can provide controlled access to public sector data to accredited users, being accredited state and federal government entities and public Australian universities, for specific purposes in the public interest. Specifically, under Article 104, if the Commissioner reasonably believes that a person has information or a document relevant to an investigation under section 101, or to any other of the Commissioner’s regulatory functions set out in section 45, the Commissioner may, by written notice given to the person, require the person to give the information, or produce the document, to the Commissioner within the period specified in the notice.

The Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979 allows law enforcement to intercept user communications, even without a warrant in emergencies or for capability testing. It details various warrants like interception (Section 10) and stored communications (Section 110). The Telecommunications and Other Legislation Amendment (Assistance and Access) Act 2018 provides industry assistance powers, ensuring cooperation with executing these warrants. However, Section 317ZH states these powers are void if they'd require a warrant under other mentioned acts. The Telecommunications Legislation Amendment (International Production Orders) Act 2020 permits the government to retrieve encrypted data from foreign companies. The Telecommunications Regulations 2021 enables cooperation between telecommunications providers, financial institutions and state agencies to reduce cyber risks, including via personal information sharing.

The Telecommunications Act 1997, as amended in 2018, provides for three types of requests or notices that the government and certain security and law enforcement agencies can issue to communications providers

Australia.act_norm: criminal code amendment act 2019 online safety act 2021 online safety determination 2022
Australia.impact:
The Criminal Code Amendment (Sharing of Abhorrent Violent Material) Act 2019, which was enacted in the wake of the Christchurch attack on 2019, subjects Internet service providers and content providers to criminal penalties for the failure to report or remove "abhorrent violent material" that the providers have reasonable grounds to believe records or streams abhorrent violent conduct in Australia (Section 434.33-34).

The Online Safety Act 2021 has significant implications for online service providers because it makes them more accountable for the online safety of the people who use their service. The Act gives eSafety substantial new powers to protect all Australians across most online platforms and forums where people can experience harm. The Act also requires industry to develop new codes to regulate illegal and restricted content. This refers to the most seriously harmful material, such as videos showing sexual abuse of children or acts of terrorism, through to content that is inappropriate for children, such as high impact violence and nudity. If the harmful content or post is not removed within 24 hours, a fine of up to 500 penalty units will be applied — for individuals, up to $111,000 and for organisations up to $555,000.

The Basic Online Safety Expectations 2022 (BOSE) is a mechanism for the Commonwealth Government to “articulate its expectations of online services providers, on behalf of the community, to improve protections for users” and that “service providers are best placed to identify emerging forms of harmful end-user conduct or material, and choose the best way to address them on their service, in the most responsive way”. The BOSE establishes a higher standard for online service providers, designed to ensure that certain online service providers prioritise online safety by imposing on them minimum standards for pre-emptive and preventative action to minimise harm that can occur via their online services, such as cyber-bullying or exposure to illegal or restricted material.

Australia.act: Criminal Code Amendment (Sharing of Abhorrent Violent Material) Act 2019
Australia.impact:
The Criminal Code Amendment (Sharing of Abhorrent Violent Material) Act 2019, subjects internet service providers, hosting and content providers to criminal penalties for the failure to report or remove "abhorrent violent material" that the providers have reasonable grounds to believe records or streams abhorrent violent conduct in Australia (Section 434.33-34).

The two key offences are:

- Failure to report—an offence for internet service providers, hosting and content providers that fail to notify the Australian Federal Police (AFP) within a reasonable time about material relating to abhorrent violent conduct occurring in Australia. A defence is available if the service provider can show that they had reasonable grounds to believe the AFP was already aware of the details of the material.

- Failure to remove—offences for content service and hosting services that fail to remove access to abhorrent violent material expeditiously where that material is reasonably capable of being accessed within Australia. The Act provides a range of defences to this offence. For example, in circumstances where it relates to law enforcement purposes, news and current affairs, public policy advocacy and research purposes.
Australia.timeframe: Since April 2019

Australia.coverage: Telecommunications; Internet services; Internet host services
Australia.impact:
It is prohibited for Internet service providers (ISPs)  and telecommunications services providers, acting on their own, to monitor and disclose the content of communications without the customer’s consent under the Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979 (Section 7). Such entities may monitor communications under the Telecommunications and Other Legislation Amendment (Assistance and Access) Act 2018 when required to assist certain law enforcement or national security agencies in carrying out their duties. The entities are also able to, but not required to, monitor their networks to carry out "network protection duties." 

Although there is no general monitoring requirement on telecommunications and Internet service providers, the Criminal Code Amendment (Sharing of Abhorrent Violent Conduct) Act 2019 provides that "[a] person commits an offence if:
(a)  the person provides a hosting service; and
(b)  material is hosted on the hosting service; and
(c)  the material is abhorrent violent material; and
(d)  the person does not expeditiously cease hosting the material (Section 474.34(5)). 

Although the Act does not impose a duty to monitor content, as for "abhorrent violent conduct", it implies that Internet hosts need to put reasonable efforts to monitor and disable such materials.

Australia.impact: Australia enacted an Online Safety Act that places additional responsibilities on digital platforms and internet service providers (ISPs) to remove harmful content posted on their services. Specifically, the Act reduces the time a site owner or ISP has to remove harmful content from 48 hours to 24 hours when served with a removal notice by the eSafety Commissioner. It also provides the eSafety Commissioner additional information collection powers and the power to require ISPs to disable access to material depicting violent conduct for a limited period during “crisis situations.”

Australia.impact: For contents implicating copyright infringements, the Copyright Act 1968 allows the owner of a copyright to request for an injunction against carriage service providers, including search engine providers, providing access to online locations outside Australia to take reasonable steps to disable access to the online locations that implicate copyright infringements (Section 115A). This section was inserted into the Copyright Act in June 2015. The Freedom House reported in 2019 that "[o]owners of copyrighted material periodically obtain orders from the Federal Court of Australia blocking copyright-infringing websites. In a decision of the Federal Court in April 2020, orders were made requiring carriage service providers to block a range of copyright-infringing websites, the majority of which are pirate torrenting and streaming websites."

Australia.act_norm: defence trade controls act 2012 customs regulations 1958 defence and strategic goods list 2024
Australia.coverage: Defence and Strategic Goods List (DSGL) and goods containing DSGL technology
Australia.impact:
The Defence Trade Controls Act 2012 lays out Australia’s export control regime for intangible technology listed on the Defence and Strategic Goods List (DSGL). The DSGL includes technologies which are required for the development, production or use of dual-use goods and munitions listed in the DSGL. Previously, controls were only imposed on tangible technology exports related to the DSGL, but the new act goes further by imposing controls on the exportation of intangible technology related to the DSGL. The Customs Regulations 1958 Regulation 13E provides that "[t]he exportation from Australia of [the goods specified in DSGL and goods containing DSGL technology are] prohibited. Regulation 13EA sets out some exceptions to this export prohibition. The DSGL has been updated and came into effect in August 2024.

Specifically, Section 5 of the List refers to Telecommunication systems and equipment, and specially designed components and accessories, advanced capabilities across encryption, resistance to extreme temperatures/radiation, advanced radios across certain spectrums and channel cycling speeds, jamming or other interception technology, counter explosive equipment, network communication surveillance technology used by law enforcement and systems to defeat or bypass information security.

The standard-setting process is a collaborative and a transparent process. It includes a wide range of stakeholders (international, private/public sector) and the treatment of intellectual property (IP) issues. For example, the Technical Committee, responsible for developments of new standards include representatives from government agencies, business, and industry organizations and associations. 

To regulate electrical products, the Australian Communications and Media Authority (ACMC) derive technical standards from Standards Alliance, a non-government and not-for-profit standards organization, if not from international standard bodies or national regulatory agencies. In developing standards, Standards Alliance form technical committees which can consist of technical, business, academic, government, and community experts as representatives from nominating organizations. According to the Structure and Operation of Standardisation Committees (SG-002) and Nominating Organization Guide, to be eligible as a nominating organization, it must have its headquarters based in Australia, have an Australian membership base, and represent a constituency among other requirements. SG-002 was published in 2001 and recently amended in June 2023.

Australia.impact:
To ensure compliance with the technical regulatory arrangements, suppliers must make and hold a Declaration of Conformity, the product of which falls under the jurisdiction of The Australian Communications and Media Authority (ACMC). The document should be signed by the Australian supplier or overseas manufacturer to certify that the product meets applicable standards. It must be signed by a person who holds a senior position in the company or organisation. The signatory should have sighted the evidence that supports the declaration and be satisfied of the grounds for compliance. It should be made available by the supplier for audit purposes on request, in writing, from either the Australian Communications Authority (ACA) or the Radio Spectrum Management Group (RSM) of the New Zealand Ministry of Economic Development Electrical products become subject to testing requirements when exported. 

To become Registered Responsible Suppliers and sell In-scope electrical equipment in EESS participating jurisdictions, all Responsible Suppliers must register in the EESS Registration Database, make a Responsible Suppliers Declaration that their In-scope equipment is electrically safe, meets all relevant standards and mark the equipment with the Regulatory Compliance Mark (RCM). In addition to the Responsible Supplier Declaration, Registered Responsible Suppliers (or Authorised Representative) must also make Equipment Declarations for Level 2 and Level 3 electrical equipment. The Equipment Declaration is specific to Level 2 and Level 3 in-scope electrical equipment and is made when equipment of this type is registered on the database. These declarations include statements that equipment meets Level-specific relevant standards in a way that is required under the EESS.

The Telecommunications Act 1997 regulates telecommunications equipment. The Radiocommunications Act 1992 regulates radiocommunications equipment, electrical or electronic household products, and radio transmitters. The ACMC allows a self declaration of conformity (SDoC) as long as the declaration contains all of the information in Australia's Declaration of Conformity (Form C02) and acknowledges that the product complies with the ACMC standards. Whoever signs the SDoC must see the evidence that the product complies with the rules and agree that the records show that the product complies with the rules. A supplier may be required to submit a separate test report from a designated testing body as well.

Australian Signals Directorate Cryptographic Evaluation

Australia.impact:
Under the Intelligence Services Act 2001, Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) conducts Cryptographic Evaluation. This evaluation regime was implemented in July 2019. Products interacting with Australian government systems and critical security environments, including the handling of classified data must use ASD Approved Cryptographic Algorithms and Protocols, in line with international standards.

ASD conducts "a combination of open source and in-house tests to ensure the correct implementation of cryptographic algorithms and protocols as well as assessing the quality of the surrounding cryptographic architecture." ASD Approved Cryptographic Algorithms are mostly internationally recognized or approved by National Institute of Standards and Technology. Such algorithms fall into three categories: asymmetric/public key algorithms, hashing algorithms, and symmetric encryption algorithms. 

The approved asymmetric/public key algorithms are: Diffie-Hellman (DH) for agreeing on encryption session keys, Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA) for digital signatures, Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH) for key exchange, Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm (ECDSA) for digital signatures, and Rivest-Shamir-Adleman (RSA) for digital signatures and passing encryption session keys or similar keys.
The approved hashing algorithm is Secure Hashing Algorithm 2 (SHA-2) (i.e. SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-384 and SHA-512).
The approved symmetric encryption algorithms are Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) using key lengths of 128, 192 and 256 bits.

Malaysia.act_norm: financial procedure act 1957
Malaysia.impact:
The Financial Procedure Act (Act 61) 1957, remains Malaysia's main legal instrument for financial  matters including government procurement. For specific works, if local contractors do not have the expertise and capability, tenders may be called on a joint venture basis between local and foreign contractors to encourage the transfer of technology. International tenders for works may only be called when local contractors do not have the expertise and capability, and a joint venture is not possible.

Section 6: Management and control of public finances, including the supervision of all matters related to the financial affairs of the Federation. This section provides overarching guidance for the use of public funds in procurement activities, including ICT goods and digital services.

Section 13: No money shall be withdrawn from the Consolidated Fund without proper authority, which also implies that all procurement (including ICT-related services) would require authorization under public financial management rules.

Section 15A: Controlling officers are responsible for ensuring that public procurement follows the prescribed rules and that expenditures are justified.

Section 36: The regulations made under this Act may provide additional guidance on procurement rules, including preferences or exclusions for certain sectors like ICT.

Malaysia.act: Government Contracts Act (Act 120) 1949
Malaysia.act_norm: government contracts act 1949
Malaysia.impact:
The Government Contracts Act (Act 120) 1949 outlines the authority and procedures for making contracts on behalf of the Government of Malaysia, as well as state governments. According to Section 2, government contracts must be made in writing and signed by an authorized minister or public officer. This framework provides the legal structure for government procurement, including potentially any exclusions related to specific sectors such as ICT. Exemptions and provisions for handling contracts under state and federal government could allow specific exclusions based on strategic or national security concerns, especially in ICT and digital services procurement

Section 2: Contracts made on behalf of the Government of Malaysia must be in writing and signed by a Minister or a public officer authorized by a Minister. This covers all government contracts, including procurement. If specific exclusions for ICT goods and services are issued, they would be aligned with this section for signing and enforcement.

Section 3: Similar provisions for State Government contracts, signed by the Chief Minister or a public officer duly authorized by the Chief Minister.

Section 5: Contracts made outside Malaysia must also adhere to this structure when made on behalf of the Government.

Malaysia.impact:
The Financial Procedure Act 1957 or related procurement documents in Malaysia do not explicitly cover requirements regarding source codes, encryption, or trade secrets in public procurement processes.

However, clauses regarding sensitive information such as “official secrets” are governed by the Official Secrets Act No.88 1972, particularly where government information or technology may be classified. Any handling of sensitive technology like encryption or software might fall under this umbrella, but no specific public procurement clauses directly address these aspects.

Section 2 - Interpretation: Defines “official secret” broadly, allowing trade secrets, encryption data, and source codes to be classified as “Top Secret,” “Secret,” “Confidential,” or “Restricted” if related to government contracts.
Section 3 - Penalties for Spying: If such sensitive information is used for espionage or acts prejudicial to Malaysia’s safety, penalties can include life imprisonment.
Section 8 - Wrongful Communication of Official Secrets: Prohibits the unauthorized communication or use of official secrets, which could include sensitive procurement information like source codes and encryption protocols, with penalties of 1 to 7 years of imprisonment.

From the UNCAC Malaysia also has preference schemes for Bumiputera suppliers, giving them priority in government procurement, particularly under the Bumiputera Commercial and Industrial Community initiatives, which provide further support for local contractors over foreign bidders. While Malaysia’s procurement system has principles of public accountability and transparency, the presence of internal evaluation committees, discretionary decision-making by Agency Procurement Boards, and the fact that only certain tender details are published online (such as the name of the successful bidder, but not full details) could point to limited transparency in the overall decision-making process. Malaysia.act: Ministerial Functions Act (Act 2) 1969
Malaysia.act_norm: ministerial functions act 1969
Malaysia.impact:
Foreign investments in services, whether in fully liberalized or controlled sub-sectors, are subject to review and approval by ministries and agencies with jurisdiction over the relevant sectors. A key function of this review and approval process is to determine whether proposed investments meet the government's qualifications for the various incentives in place to promote economic development goals. The Ministerial Functions Act grants relevant ministries broad discretionary powers over the approval of investment projects.  Investors in industries targeted by the Malaysian government can often negotiate favorable terms with the ministries or agencies responsible for regulating that industry.  This can include assistance in navigating a complex web of regulations and policies, some of which can be waived on a case-by-case basis. Foreign investors in non-targeted industries tend to receive less government assistance in obtaining the necessary approvals from various regulatory bodies and therefore can face greater bureaucratic obstacles.

Ministerial Functions Act (Act 2) 1969 provides the legal framework for ministries to regulate and control foreign investments, ensuring alignment with Malaysia’s economic development goals through a discretionary approval process. The Act serves as a form of screening mechanism, allowing the government to selectively support or restrict foreign investments based on national priorities.

Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 (CMA)
- Section 126: Licensing of network facilities, services, and applications services. Licenses are subject to the approval of the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC), and transfer or acquisition of licenses is regulated to ensure compliance with national objectives.

- Section 34: Implieas Foreign Investment Restrictions. Renewal and transfer conditions for individual licenses. Licenses require ministerial approval for renewal, transfer, or any ownership changes, ensuring that the government retains oversight on critical sectors.

- Section 233: Regulatory Power to require disclosure of ownership or control. The MCMC has authority to investigate and assess ownership structures, ensuring that any investment aligns with Malaysia’s strategic goals.

Malaysia.act: Communications and Multimedia Act (Act 588) 1998
Malaysia.act_norm: communications and multimedia act 1998
Malaysia.impact:
Malaysia does not have a centralized foreign direct investment (FDI) screening procedure applicable across all sectors. Instead, foreign investment is regulated on a sector-specific basis, with oversight from relevant ministries or regulatory authorities. The Ministerial Functions Act 1969 applies broadly across many sectors and grants ministries the ability to negotiate terms with foreign investors, but it is not the primary legislation governing telecommunications. Therefore, while the Act establishes a horizontal screening mechanism, telecommunications investment reviews are handled separately through the MCMC’s regulatory framework

Some key sectors have restrictions on foreign equity participation and specific screening mechanisms: Foreign ownership is limited, typically requiring a maximum foreign equity cap of 30% in broadcasting services. The Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) oversees investments in these sectors and applies specific scrutiny to mergers and acquisitions involving license holders under the Communications and Multimedia Act (Act 588) 1998 (CMA). 

Section 126, allow MCMC to intervene in mergers and acquisitions that could lead to substantial foreign control, ensuring that these investments do not compromise national sovereignty or public security. For licensees under the CMA, MCMC can conduct reviews to assess whether mergers or acquisitions may result in a substantial lessening of competition or pose a national security risk. Although notification is voluntary, the merger control regime enables MCMC to monitor foreign investments closely.

Malaysia.act:
Patents Act (Act 291) 1983;

Patents (Amendment) Act (Act A1649) 2022
Malaysia.act_norm: patents act 1983 patents act 2022
Malaysia.impact:
There are differential treatment relevant to patent applications in Malaysia. According to the Patents Act (Act 291) 1983, patent applicants who are not residents of Malaysia are explicitly required to appoint a local patent agent to act on their behalf. This is stipulated under the requirement for patent agents in Section 86(5), where non-residents must appoint local representatives to file and manage their applications.

Section 29A legal language stresses the need for all patent applications to comply with procedural and substantive examination requirements. If the applicant does not request a substantive or modified substantive examination within the required time period, the application is deemed withdrawn. If any discrimination occurred, it would likely be an issue of practice, not law.

Malaysia has a formal process for patent applications, including a preliminary and substantive examination. The transparency of the process is supported by the publication of patent applications in the Official Journal, as mandated under the Patents (Amendment) Act (Act A1649) 2022.

The Patents Act (Act 291) 1983 includes a requirement for residents of Malaysia to file patent applications domestically before filing abroad. Specifically, Section 23A of the Act mandates that residents must file a patent in Malaysia and wait two months before applying for a patent for the same invention abroad. This regulation ensures that the Malaysian government retains control over the disclosure of inventions before they are filed internationally.

Since March 2022

Malaysia.act:
Patents Act (Act 291) 1983;

Patents (Amendment) Act (Act A1649) 2022
Malaysia.act_norm: patents act 1983 patents act 2022
Malaysia.impact:
A patent owner has the right to initiate court proceedings (Section 59) against anyone who infringes or is likely to infringe the patent. This includes actions against actual infringement as well as “imminent infringement”, where acts make it likely that an infringement will occur.

Civil remedies such as injunctions (Section 60) and damages can be sought. The court may award damages to the patent owner and grant an injunction or  interim injunctions to prevent ongoing or imminent infringement and further infringement. Infringement proceedings must be initiated within five years of the infringing act to ensure timely action. Licensees and compulsory license beneficiaries also have the right to initiate infringement proceedings (Section 61), provided certain conditions are met.

The Registrar of Patents can grant a compulsory license (Section 51) if the patent has not been adequately exploited in Malaysia (Section 49). After three years from the patent grant, or four years from the filing date, a third party may apply for a compulsory license under certain circumstances, such as insufficient production or unreasonable pricing. The process of obtaining a compulsory license involves proving the absence of legitimate reasons for not working the patent, which may present a high burden for applicants.

Enforcement officers (Section 70) authorized by the Ministry have the power to conduct searches and seizures as part of patent enforcement efforts. This includes the ability to seize goods that are suspected of infringing on patents. In Section 69 Officers may be authorized to arrest individuals involved in patent infringement if necessary, demonstrating Malaysia’s robust enforcement mechanisms for intellectual property rights.

Malaysia.act:
Patents Act (Act 291) 1983;

Patents (Amendment) Act (Act A1649) 2022
Malaysia.act_norm: patents act 1983 patents act 2022
Malaysia.impact:
There is no practical or legal restriction on the patent enforcement found. Under the Patents (Amendment) Act 2022,  patent applicants can now request for restoration of the right of priority if the failure to act was unintentional. Moreover, Malaysia has a specialized Intellectual Property High Court in Kuala Lumpur where most civil actions for patent infringement are filed.

The patent owner has the right to bring a legal action against any individual or entity that infringes on their patent (Section 59). This includes cases of actual infringement and imminent infringement. The proceedings must be initiated within five years of the infringing act which can be a detrimental statutory limitation. This restriction affects all sectors and applies broadly to patent enforcement. Missing this deadline may entirely prevent a patent holder from seeking remedies.

A patent holder may choose to surrender their patent by notifying the Registrar (Sections 55 and 56). Furthermore, any party can seek to invalidate a patent if they can prove that the invention is not novel, inventive, or is excluded from patentability under Malaysian law.

Malaysia.act:
Copyright Right Act (Act 332) 1987;

Copyright Right (Amendment) Act (Act A1645) 2022
Malaysia.act_norm: copyright right act 1987 copyright right act 2022
Malaysia.impact:
The Copyright Right Act (Act 332) 1987 includes fair dealing as a copyright exception for purposes of research, private study, criticism, review or the reporting of news or current events. In determining whether an act constitutes a fair dealing, the factors to be considered include: 
- the purpose and character of the dealing, including whether such dealing is of a commercial nature or is for non-profit educational purposes;
- the nature of the copyright work;
- the amount and substantiality of the portion used in relation to the copyright work as a whole; and 
- the effect of the dealing upon the potential market for or value of the copyright work.

Malaysia’s system sets “Clear Copyright Exceptions Following Fair Use or Fair Dealing Model”, as the country’s fair dealing provisions are well-defined and structured. 

The Copyright Right (Amendment) Act (Act A1645) 2022 introduced the concept of collective management organizations (CMOs) to replace the term “licensing bodies.” CMOs are responsible for managing and enforcing copyright on behalf of authors, performers, and rights holders. However, the reliance on CMOs may present a gap in individual enforcement, as individual rights holders might face challenges in enforcing their rights if the CMO is not functioning effectively. The legislation allows CMOs to be declared inactive or revoked if they fail to operate properly.

Section 36A addresses technological protection measures (TPMs), which are designed to prevent unauthorized copying or access to digital content. The act prohibits circumvention of TPMs unless certain exceptions apply, such as for law enforcement, research, or interoperability of software. 

Both the 2012 and 2022 amendments expand fair dealing provisions to include educational, research, and private study purposes, allowing copyrighted materials to be used without explicit permission from the copyright owner. This includes exceptions for research, criticism, review, and the reporting of news.

A major focus of the 2022 amendment is the Marrakesh Treaty, which facilitates access to published works for people with print disabilities. The amendment introduces provisions allowing authorized entities and individuals to create and distribute accessible format copies of copyrighted works without infringing on copyright. While it’s an important social and humanitarian exception, it reduces the level of control for authors and publishers over how their works are reproduced and distributed.

The Copyright (Amendment) Act 2022 introduces a system for the compounding of offences under Section 41A, allowing certain copyright offences to be settled through payment without going to trial. While this expedites enforcement, it might be seen as a gap by rights holders who want stronger penalties for copyright infringement rather than allowing offenders to settle through payments.

Malaysia.act_norm: copyright right act 1987 copyright right act 2022
Malaysia.impact:
Copyright (Amendment) Act (Act A1645) 2022, Malaysia has implemented a structured framework for civil and administrative procedures, remedies, and provisional measures for online copyright enforcement. The country has a clear notice-and-takedown system, the ability to seek injunctions, and provisional measures like compounding of offences.
- Notice-and-Takedown System (Section 43H of the 2012 Amendment): Copyright owners can notify Internet Service Providers (ISPs) of infringing content, requiring the ISP to remove or disable access to the infringing material within 48 hours. If the service provider fails to comply, they can be held liable for the infringement.
- Counter-Notification (Section 43H(3)): The party whose content was removed can submit a counter-notification if they believe the removal was unjustified. This process requires ISPs to restore access to the content unless the original copyright owner takes legal action to block the restoration.
- Injunctions (Section 41 of the 2022 Amendment): The Act empowers courts to issue injunctions against copyright infringers to prevent ongoing violations. This applies to both physical and digital infringements.
- Compounding of Offences (Section 41A): Certain copyright offences may be compounded, allowing the infringer to settle the matter by paying a fine rather than facing prosecution, streamlining enforcement procedures.

Criminal Procedure Code (Act 593) 2018;

Trade Descriptions Act (Act 730) 2011
Malaysia.act_norm: criminal procedure code 2018 trade descriptions act 2011
Malaysia.impact:
Section 116B(1) of the Criminal Procedure Code (Act 593) provides police officers with a warrant from a magistrate with the power to be given access to encrypted data, including through the provision of a “necessary password, encryption code, decryption code, software or hardware and any other means required to enable comprehension of the computerised data”. Failure to comply is an offence punishable by imprisonment or a fine. 

This section grants significant authority to law enforcement to gain access to encrypted data, which could include sensitive information like communications, business data, or personal details stored digitally. It is particularly important in criminal investigations involving digital evidence. While this law grants access, it only applies with a warrant from a magistrate, meaning it is subject to judicial oversight. The requirement of a warrant provides some level of protection against arbitrary access to sensitive data.

In Malaysia, trade secrets, including source code and algorithms, are generally protected under common law principles of confidentiality, as the country does not have a dedicated Trade Secrets Act. Malaysia does not impose mandatory disclosure of trade secrets like source code or algorithms in any general or specific legal framework. Trade secrets are protected under common law principles of confidentiality and through contractual agreements, such as non-disclosure agreements (NDAs) or confidentiality clauses. However, in the Trade Descriptions Act (Act 730) 2011 (Sections 34, 35, 43) gives authorities the power to access and seize computerized data, including any necessary decryption information, for the purpose of enforcing the Act. While confidentiality is protected, there are exceptions that allow disclosure when required for legal or enforcement proceedings.

Malaysia.act:
Trade Descriptions Act (Act 730) 2011;

Criminal Procedure Code (Act 593) 2018
Malaysia.act_norm: trade descriptions act 2011 criminal procedure code 2018
Malaysia.impact:
In Malaysia, trade secrets and confidential information are protected by the common law tort of breach of confidential information and/or by contract. Trade secrets are protected as long as they consistently meets the eligibility for being confidential information. According to Art. 35, any person who discloses or makes use of any confidential information or document with respect to a particular enterprise or the affairs of an individual obtained by virtue of any provision of this Act commits an offence. The law defines confidential information as trade, business or industrial information that belongs to any person, that has economic value and is not generally available to or known by others. 

Malaysia does not have a dedicated trade secrets law like some countries, but trade secrets are protected through common law principles (e.g., breach of confidence) and contractual agreements (such as NDAs). There are also specific clauses in laws like the Criminal Procedure Code  (Act 593) 2018 (Section 116B) and Trade Descriptions Act (Act 730) 2011 that can mandate the disclosure of trade secrets (such as source code or algorithms) in certain legal or regulatory situations, particularly during criminal investigations or enforcement actions. These provisions indicate that while protection exists, it is limited and subject to legal proceeding exceptions.

There is no clear, uniform definition of what constitutes a trade secret (e.g., source code, algorithms) across all sectors. Current protections rely on common law principles (e.g., breach of confidence) and contract law (NDAs), which may not offer sufficient protection or remedies in cases of misappropriation. A specific law would offer clear, enforceable remedies for trade secret theft. The current framework is sector-specific (e.g., criminal or regulatory proceedings). A dedicated law would provide consistent, broad protection across industries without needing a specific legal or regulatory trigger for enforcement.

Thus, Malaysia provides some level of trade secret protection through various legal means, but it is not comprehensive or specifically tailored like in jurisdictions with dedicated trade secret laws.

Public Inquiry Report on the Review of the Mandatory Standard on Access from September 2022, infrastructure sharing in Malaysia, particularly passive infrastructure, is addressed in the Mandatory Standard on Access (MSA) under the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Act (Act 588)  1998 (CMA). The report emphasizes the importance of sharing network elements, infrastructure, and co-location services to avoid duplication and ensure efficient use of existing facilities.

Section 25 of the Report outlines the specific obligations for infrastructure sharing, which encourages service providers to share physical infrastructure like towers, ducts, and poles. Section 26 addresses co-location services, which allow operators to share physical space on infrastructure owned by other operators, further promoting passive sharing.

Malaysia.act_norm: communications and multimedia act 1998 guidelines on implementation of accounting separation in malaysia 2016
acts_norm: guidelines on implementation of accounting separation in malaysia 2016
Malaysia.impact:
Operators with both revenues and total assets in a fiscal year exceeding RM3 billion (Section 2.1) respectively are subject to full accounting separation obligations while operators with either or both revenues and total assets below RM3 billion respectively are subjected to simplified accounting separation obligations.

The Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) mandates that all telecommunications operators implement Accounting Separation (AS) to ensure transparency in the financial interactions (Section 1.1) between different services and business units. Operators are required to submit Regulatory Financial Statements (RFS) annually. Operators must categorize costs (direct, directly attributable, indirectly attributable) and attribute them to services based on the principle of cost causation. They must also use transfer charges (market prices or cost-plus capital) to account for internal transactions between business units. (Section 5.4 and Section 6.1). 

There is no formal requirement for functional separation under the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 (CMA) or MCMC guidelines. Functional separation would typically involve structural changes to separate the operational management of network facilities and services from retail or competitive service units.

Malaysia.impact:
The Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) is an independent statutory body established under the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission Act (Act 589) 1998. 

The MCMC operates independently, though it is subject to the overall policies set by the Malaysian government. It is responsible for ensuring fair competition, managing spectrum allocation, enforcing regulatory compliance, and overseeing infrastructure development, such as broadband and mobile networks. Under Section 16, the Commission shall have all the functions imposed on
it under the communications and multimedia laws and, without prejudice to the generality of the foregoing, the Commission shall
also have the following functions, for example, (e) to supervise and monitor communications and multimedia activities, and (h) to promote and maintain the integrity of all persons licensed.

Malaysia.act_norm: personal data protection act 2010 personal data protection bill 2024 personal data protection code of practice for banking sector and financial institutions 2017
acts_norm: personal data protection code of practice for banking sector and financial institutions 2017
Malaysia.impact:
The Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010 (PDPA) prohibits the transfer of personal data out of Malaysia unless such transfer is to a country, which has been specified and recorded in the Official Gazette by the Minister. Currently, no countries have been specified officially. Notwithstanding the prohibition on transfers of personal data out of the country, the PDPA sets out a number of exceptions to the prohibition, such as where the consent of the data subject has been obtained for such transfer and where the transfer is necessary for the performance of a contract between the parties. When in doubt as to whether the exemptions on data transfer apply, the prudent approach would be to obtain consent from the data subject in respect of such out of Malaysia transfer. In relation to outsourcing, a data user is not allowed to share data with third parties unless the consent of the individual has been obtained.

The PDPA applies broadly across sectors to regulate the processing of personal data for commercial transactions. Any personal data processed in Malaysia must adhere to PDPA standards, including specific security and privacy principles. Section 6 requires that personal data should not be processed unless the data subject consents, or it is necessary for specific legal reasons. The Security Principle in Section 9 enforces practical steps to protect personal data from unauthorized or accidental access during processing, which aligns with local data storage requirements. Section 129 of the PDPA mentions restrictions on transferring personal data to places outside Malaysia unless the destination has data protection laws or the transfer meets specific conditions.

The Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for the Banking and Financial Sector 2017 mandates that personal data be processed according to PDPA principles. Specific rules about data transfer abroad are mentioned in the document, limiting cross-border data transfers without compliance. Similar requirements for the Communications Sector are set out in the Code of Practice for Licensees Under the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998, especially for network and service providers. Both banking and communications sectors mention compliance with the Data Transfer Abroad clause, ensuring that any cross-border data transfer adheres to Malaysia’s data protection standards duscussed in other relevant subpillars.

Malaysia.act_norm: personal data protection act 2010 personal data protection bill 2024 personal data protection code of practice for banking sector and financial institutions 2017
acts_norm: personal data protection code of practice for banking sector and financial institutions 2017
Malaysia.impact:
Malaysia currently does not have a strict data localization law that mandates all personal data to be stored exclusively within the country. However, the Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010 includes provisions that regulate cross-border data transfers, requiring that personal data can only be transferred out of Malaysia if the recipient country provides an adequate level of data protection. This means that while data can be stored and processed abroad, certain safeguards must be in place to protect personal data.

For certain sectors, like banking and finance, there are sector-specific guidelines (e.g., the Banking and Financial Sector Code of Practice) that impose more stringent conditions on data transfers and suggest that local processing is preferable to ensure compliance with security and privacy standards. These requirements do not constitute a blanket local storage mandate but make local storage a practical solution for ensuring compliance with security obligations, especially for sensitive data.

Malaysia.act: Services Tax Act (Act 807) 2018
Malaysia.act_norm: services tax act 2018
Malaysia.impact:
Section 24 of the Service Tax Act 2018 (Act 807) sets out the obligations for recordkeeping by taxable persons. Every taxable person must keep complete, accurate, and up-to-date records of all transactions that affect or may affect their liability to service tax. This includes records of all taxable services provided or received (such as invoices, receipts, debit and credit notes), records of imported taxable services, and any other records as may be determined by the Director General. These records must be preserved for seven years from the most recent date to which they relate, must be kept in either the national language or English, and must be retained in Malaysia unless otherwise approved by the Director General under specific conditions.

If records are maintained in an electronically readable format, they must be stored in a way that allows for ready access and conversion into writing. If records originally kept in manual form are later converted to electronic form, the original manual versions must also be preserved. Copies of records are admissible in legal proceedings to the same extent as the originals. This section also applies to any person, other than a taxable person, who acquires imported taxable services in the course of business. Failure to comply with any of these requirements is an offence and may result in a fine of up to RM50,000, imprisonment of up to three years, or both. However, these obligations do not apply to foreign registered persons.

Note that this requirement was available under the repealed Goods and Services Tax Act 2014.

Malaysia.act: Income Tax Act (Act 53) 1967
Malaysia.act_norm: income tax act 1967
Malaysia.impact:
Section 82 imposes a legal duty on all businesses to maintain proper records and, where applicable, issue receipts. Every business must keep accurate and sufficient records for at least seven years from the end of the relevant income year to allow tax authorities to determine taxable income or losses. If gross takings exceed RM150,000 from goods or RM100,000 from services, the business must issue serially numbered printed receipts for each transaction and keep duplicates. Businesses that file late returns must also keep records for seven years from the filing date. 

If a sales recording machine is used, the receipt requirement may be waived, but only if the machine records all transactions accurately. The Director General may set or waive requirements for recordkeeping and receipts and may also request audited financial statements under the Companies Act 2016 if existing records are inadequate. Entries must be made within 60 days of each transaction. Records kept electronically must be readable, accessible, and convertible to written form; if manual records are later digitized, the originals must be preserved.

Moreover, All records that relate to any business in Malaysia shall be kept and retained in Malaysia. This includes records of (a) books of account recording receipts and payments or income and expenditure; (b) invoices, vouchers, receipts and such other documents as in the opinion of the Director General are necessary to verify the entries in any books of account; and (c) any other records as may be specified by the Director General under subsection (3).

Malaysia.act_norm: personal data protection act 2010 personal data protection bill 2024
Malaysia.impact:
Malaysia has a comprehensive data protection framework established through the Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010, which applies to the processing of personal data in commercial transactions. The PDPA regulates how personal data can be collected, used, and disclosed, and it imposes obligations on data users (companies and organizations) to protect the personal data of individuals.

Moreover, the Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Bill 2024 (Act A1727) amendments to the PDPA further enhance this framework by introducing mandatory data breach notifications, extending data processor obligations, and increasing penalties for non-compliance, aligning Malaysia’s privacy regulations more closely with international standards like the EU’s GDPR. Specifically, data Breach Notification (Section 12b) requires data controllers (including those in banking and communications sectors) to notify the Personal Data Protection Commissioner and affected data subjects in the event of a data breach, especially if the breach poses significant harm. Moreover, penalties (Amendment of Section 5) prescribes that the penalties for non-compliance with the Security Principle or data breach requirements have been increased to 1 million ringgit or imprisonment of up to three years, or both.

In addition to the PDPA, sector-specific codes of practice also exist for industries like banking, finance, and communications, but these operate within the comprehensive legal framework provided by the PDPA.

acts_norm: personal data protection code of practice for banking sector and financial institutions 2017
Malaysia.impact:
Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for the Banking and Financial Sector 2017

- Article 3.1: Data Collection and Use: Banks must collect personal data only for purposes directly related to their operational functions, such as account management, financial transactions, and compliance with regulatory requirements. Explicit consent from the customer is mandatory before data collection.
- Article 4.1: Security of Personal Data. Banking institutions must implement technical safeguards, such as encryption and firewalls, and organizational measures, including restricted access, to ensure data protection. Regular audits and security reviews are required to address evolving threats.
- Article 5.1: Retention and Disposal of Data. Personal data must not be retained longer than necessary. The retention period should align with legal or regulatory requirements (e.g., Anti-Money Laundering laws). Secure deletion and destruction processes must be in place when data is no longer needed.
- Article 6.2: Data Subject Access and Correction
Customers have the right to:
	• Request access to their data.
	• Request corrections to inaccuracies in their data.
	• Banks must respond within a reasonable timeframe and may charge reasonable fees for processing such requests.
- Article 7.2: Sharing Data with Third Parties. Personal data may only be disclosed to third parties (e.g., credit reporting agencies, payment processors) with the customer’s consent or as required by law. Agreements with third parties must ensure compliance with equivalent data protection standards.
- Article 8.3: Data Breach Reporting. Banks are required to notify the relevant authorities and affected individuals in the event of a significant data breach impacting personal data.

Malaysia.act: Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for Licensees Under the Communications and Multimedia (Act 1998) of 2017
Malaysia.act_norm: personal data protection code of practice for licensees under the communications and multimedia of 2017
acts_norm: personal data protection code of practice for licensees under the communications and multimedia of 2017
Malaysia.impact:
Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for Licensees under the Communications and Multimedia (Act 1998) 2017

- Article 3.2: Notice and Choice Principle - Licensees must provide clear and accessible notices to individuals regarding the purpose of data collection, retention periods, and customer rights. Individuals must be given the choice to consent to or refuse data collection.
- Article 4.1: Limitation of Use - Personal data can only be processed for purposes that the individual has been explicitly informed of during collection, such as service provision, billing, and fraud prevention.
- Article 5.1: Security Measures -Communication service providers must ensure the confidentiality of customers’ data through the implementation of cybersecurity protocols. Measures such as encryption, user authentication, and intrusion detection systems are mandated.
- Article 6.3: Data Retention - Personal data must be stored only for as long as required for operational, billing, or legal purposes. After the purpose is fulfilled, data must be permanently deleted or anonymized to prevent misuse.
- Article 7.2: Data Access and Accuracy
Customers have the right to:
	• Request access to their personal data stored by the licensee.
	• Rectify incorrect or outdated information.
	• Licensees must facilitate these requests promptly, barring legal exceptions.
- Article 8.1: Domestic Data Transfers - Personal data shared with third parties within Malaysia must adhere to the same security and privacy standards as required by the PDPA. Agreements with third parties (e.g., contractors, service providers) must enforce compliance with this code.
- Article 9.1: Cross-Border Data Restrictions. Although primarily related to international transfers, this article emphasizes that all personal data must be processed domestically unless explicit customer consent is obtained for cross-border processing.
- Article 10.3: Breach Notification. Licensees must promptly notify affected customers and relevant regulators of any data breach compromising personal information.

Malaysia.act:
Computer Crimes Act (Act 563) 1997;

Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024
Malaysia.act_norm: computer crimes act 1997 cyber security act 2024
Malaysia.impact:
Malaysia previous cybersecurity law was based on Computer Crimes Act (Act 563) 1997, it criminalizes various cybercrimes, such as unauthorized access to computers, modification of computer content, and other malicious cyber activities. However, it mainly addresses cyber offenses rather than a broader cybersecurity framework. The Cyber Security Act 2024 now provides a comprehensive framework to address national cybersecurity threats and regulate service providers.

The new Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 enhances Malaysia’s national cybersecurity efforts by:
	1. Establishing a National Cyber Security Committee, responsible for overseeing cybersecurity policies and national coordination (Part III - Duties and Powers of Chief Executive).
	2. Defining roles for critical information infrastructure entities and mandating them to comply with cybersecurity risk assessments, audits, and notifications of cybersecurity incidents. Sections 15-24 cover the responsibilities and duties of entities designated as national critical information infrastructure.
	3. Regulating cybersecurity service providers through a licensing regime to ensure that those offering services like managed security operation center monitoring and penetration testing adhere to national standards. Sections 27-34 focus on the licensing of cybersecurity service providers. 
	4. Strengthening enforcement through powers of investigation, search, and seizure, allowing the government to respond effectively to cybersecurity threats (Sections 36-52), thus, enabling the enforcement of cybersecurity regulations and the management of cyber incidents.
This new law positions Malaysia to better handle cyber risks and threats to its critical infrastructures.

Malaysia.act: Services Tax Act (Act 807) 2018
Malaysia.act_norm: services tax act 2018
Malaysia.impact:
Section 24 of the Service Tax Act 2018 (Act 807) sets out the obligations for recordkeeping by taxable persons. Every taxable person must keep complete, accurate, and up-to-date records of all transactions that affect or may affect their liability to service tax. This includes records of all taxable services provided or received (such as invoices, receipts, debit and credit notes), records of imported taxable services, and any other records as may be determined by the Director General. These records must be preserved for seven years from the most recent date to which they relate, must be kept in either the national language or English, and must be retained in Malaysia unless otherwise approved by the Director General under specific conditions.

If records are maintained in an electronically readable format, they must be stored in a way that allows for ready access and conversion into writing. If records originally kept in manual form are later converted to electronic form, the original manual versions must also be preserved. Copies of records are admissible in legal proceedings to the same extent as the originals. This section also applies to any person, other than a taxable person, who acquires imported taxable services in the course of business. Failure to comply with any of these requirements is an offence and may result in a fine of up to RM50,000, imprisonment of up to three years, or both. However, these obligations do not apply to foreign registered persons.

Note that this requirement was available under the repealed Goods and Services Tax Act 2014.

Malaysia.act: Income Tax Act (Act 53) 1967
Malaysia.act_norm: income tax act 1967
Malaysia.impact:
Section 82 imposes a legal duty on all businesses to maintain proper records and, where applicable, issue receipts. Every business must keep accurate and sufficient records for at least seven years from the end of the relevant income year to allow tax authorities to determine taxable income or losses. If gross takings exceed RM150,000 from goods or RM100,000 from services, the business must issue serially numbered printed receipts for each transaction and keep duplicates. Businesses that file late returns must also keep records for seven years from the filing date. 

If a sales recording machine is used, the receipt requirement may be waived, but only if the machine records all transactions accurately. The Director General may set or waive requirements for recordkeeping and receipts and may also request audited financial statements under the Companies Act 2016 if existing records are inadequate. Entries must be made within 60 days of each transaction. Records kept electronically must be readable, accessible, and convertible to written form; if manual records are later digitized, the originals must be preserved.

Moreover, All records that relate to any business in Malaysia shall be kept and retained in Malaysia. This includes records of (a) books of account recording receipts and payments or income and expenditure; (b) invoices, vouchers, receipts and such other documents as in the opinion of the Director General are necessary to verify the entries in any books of account; and (c) any other records as may be specified by the Director General under subsection (3).
Malaysia.timeframe: Since September 1967, last amended in December 2018

Malaysia.act_norm: personal data protection act 2010 personal data protection bill 2024
Malaysia.impact:
The Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Bill 2024 (Act A1727) amendments to the Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010 (PDPA) introduced new requirements, including the appointment of Data Protection Officers (DPOs) and the implementation of Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs), marking significant changes compared to the original PDPA 2010. Section 12a (1) of the 2024 PDPA amendment mandates that a data controller must appoint one or more Data Protection Officers (DPOs). The DPO is accountable for ensuring compliance with the PDPA, which applies horizontally across all sectors. This is a significant change from the 2010 version, where no explicit requirement for a DPO was mandated.

The 2024 amendments also introduced a requirement for DPIAs in specific cases where data processing activities pose a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals. This is especially relevant for large-scale processing of sensitive personal data or new technologies. Similar to the DPO requirement, the DPIA requirement applies horizontally across all sectors when high-risk processing is involved. Another significant change from the 2010 PDPA, as organizations are now required to assess and mitigate risks through formal DPIA procedures.

Since November 2013, last amended in 2024; 

Since July 2024

Malaysia.act_norm: personal data protection act 2010 personal data protection bill 2024
Malaysia.impact:
From the Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Bill 2024 (Act A1727) amendments, there is no explicit provision that allows the government to access personal data without a court order. 

The Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010 PDPA Section 45(2)(a)(i) exempts personal data from the consent requirement if it is processed for the prevention or detection of crime, or for the purposes of investigations. And Section 121 grants authorized officers the power to request the production of documents or data (including computerized data) to ensure compliance with the PDPA. An authorized officer shall have the powers of a police officer of whatever rank as provided for under the Criminal Procedure Code (Act 593)  in relation to investigation, and such powers shall be in addition to and not in derogation of, the powers provided for under this Act (Section 112 Power of investigation). A Magistrate may issue a warrant for an officer to search premises and seize evidence related to an offense, with or without assistance and by force if needed (Section 113 Search and seizure with warrant). However, where delay could harm the investigation, an officer may search without a warrant using the same powers (Section 114 Search and seizure without warrant). 

These specific sectoral guidelines indicate that while court orders are generally required for accessing personal data, exceptions exist, particularly for criminal investigations and regulatory functions, where data can be accessed without consent or court intervention.

Under Section 6(3) of the Security Offenses (Special Measures) Act (Act 747) 2012 (Power to intercept communication), a police officer with the rank of superintendent of police or above may intercept communications without the authorization of the public prosecutor in urgent and sudden cases where immediate action is required leaving no moment of deliberation.

Malaysia.act_norm: criminal procedure code 2018
Malaysia.impact:
The Criminal Procedure Code (Act 593) 2018 Section 116B (Access to computerized data) of the Criminal Procedure Code provides that a police officer conducting a search under the Code must be given access to computerised data whether stored in a computer or otherwise. For the purpose of this section, 'access' includes being provided with the necessary password, encryption code, decryption code, software, or hardware and any other means required to enable comprehension of the computerised data. It is not clear whether a court order is needed to access the information. 

Additionally, Section 116C (Interception of communication) provides that the public prosecutor has the authority to authorize police officer to intercept, detain and open postal articles. This also includes intercept messages or conversations transmitted by any communication, and install devices on premises for intercepting communications. The Court shall take cognizance of any authorization by the Public Prosecutor under this section.

Copyright Right Act (Act 332) 1987;

Copyright Right (Amendment) Act (Act A1645) 2022
Malaysia.act_norm: copyright right act 1987 copyright right act 2022
Malaysia.impact:
The Copyright Right Act (Act 332) 1987 (Section 41(1)) outlines the general provisions of copyright law and introduces liability principles for various parties, including intermediaries like ISPs. However, the Copyright (Amendment) Act 2012 specifically brought in clearer safe harbor provisions for ISPs, shielding them from liability for copyright infringements occurring on their networks, provided they adhere to notice-and-takedown procedures.

The Copyright Right (Amendment) Act (Act A1645) 2022 amendments introduced significant provisions regarding Internet Service Providers (ISP) liability. These provisions provide safe harbor for ISPs, protecting them from being held liable for copyright infringements that occur on their networks if they take action, such as removing infringing material when notified.  ISPs are shielded from liability, but this can make enforcement difficult for rights holders who must rely on notice-and-takedown procedures, which may be slow or ineffective. This might be seen as a gap in enforcement because it limits the speed and scope of copyright protection on digital platforms.

Regarding intermediary liability in the Copyright (Amendment) Act 2022, it strengthens the provisions related to copyright enforcement in the digital realm. For instance, section 41 was amended to extend liability to those providing access to infringing works online. It also introduced penalties for sharing unauthorized access to copyrighted material (section 41, subparagraph (k)) and imposed stricter consequences on those facilitating infringement through streaming technology (section 43aa).

Since March 2022
