Copyright Right Act (Act 332) 1987;

Copyright Right (Amendment) Act (Act A1645) 2022
Malaysia.act_norm: copyright right act 1987 copyright right act 2022
Malaysia.impact:
- Section 43aa of the Copyright (Amendment) Act 2022 addresses illegal streaming activities regarding offences related to streaming technology, making it an offence to manufacture, import, sell, distribute, or offer streaming technology that infringes on copyright. The law does not provide a safe harbor for ISPs or other intermediaries involved in facilitating access to this technology, even if they may not be directly involved in the distribution of the infringing content.
- Section 41 of the Copyright Act 1987 (Increased liability for sharing unauthorized access to copyrighted works), as amended in 2022, explicitly extends liability to anyone providing or sharing access to an online location containing copyrighted works without authorization. This provision includes ISPs if they fail to prevent such activities or do not act when notified. This highlights the lack of safe harbor for ISPs in cases where they may indirectly facilitate access to infringing materials.

While ISPs are shielded under the notice-and-takedown system for copyright infringements, they do not have broad protections for other illegal activities such as providing access to illegal streaming technology or sharing unauthorized content. Enforcement can also be more rigorous under the amended provisions, with significant fines and penalties imposed for such violations.

Sedition (Amendment) Act (Act A 1485) 2015
Malaysia.act_norm: sedition act 1948 sedition act 2015
Malaysia.impact:
The Sedition (Amendment) Act (Act A 1485) 2015 includes several provisions that support concerns about censorship and blocking, particularly in relation to electronic communications and social media. 
- Amendment of Section 2: The definition of "by electronic means" is introduced, which includes broadcasting or communicating through network services. This broadens the scope of what can be considered seditious when shared electronically.
- Amendment of Section 4: The penalties for seditious acts are increased significantly. The amendment specifies imprisonment for a term of not less than three years but not exceeding seven years for first-time offenders, and up to twenty years if the act causes bodily injury or property damage. This increase in penalties could deter individuals from sharing content that might be perceived as seditious.
- New Section 10a: This section empowers the court to issue orders to prevent access to seditious publications made by electronic means, especially when the perpetrator cannot be identified. This provision could be used to block access to certain online content deemed seditious.

The Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Content Code regulates that both the Internet Access Service Provider (IASP) and Internet content Hosting Provider (IHC) to remove prohibited content, ranging from 1 to 24 hours from the time of notification. The prohibited content include menace content that leads public disorder, threat to national security or public safety, and bad language which is the  language that is contrary to audience expectation.

- Section 7.1.1 of IASP - Internet Access Service Providers: ISPs are permitted to block or remove access to prohibited content after being notified by the Complaints Bureau. ISPs have the right to withdraw access when a subscriber provides content that contravenes Malaysian law or the Content Code. This can include commercial web content if it is deemed illegal.

The Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Content Code regulates in detail about both electronic and non-electronic  advertisement in Malaysia. The Code include a list of unacceptable products and services such as slimming products, fire crackers, and marriage agencies, amongst others.

- Section 4.2 emphasizes the legality of advertisements, stating that advertisers are responsible for ensuring that their advertisements comply with Malaysian laws, particularly in how products and services are promoted online. The advertising content must adhere to all legal requirements and ethical codes established by professional bodies.
- Section 4.14 prohibits advertisements that promote unacceptable products or services, including those that are illegal under Malaysian law. This implies that any commercial web content that advertises prohibited items may be subject to removal or blocking

Malaysia.coverage: Content Applications Service Provider, Applications Service Provider (ASP)
Malaysia.impact:
The Communications and Multimedia Act (Act 588) 1998 outlines licensing requirements for various service providers under Part VII. Specifically, Section 126 mentions licensing for Application Service Providers (ASPs), which includes social media platforms, cloud services, and VPNs. These providers are required to obtain a license from the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) to operate legally. Additional details and guidelines are provided under Section 6 of the Act.

Communications and Multimedia Act (Act 588) 1998 (CMA) 
Part IV: Licenses
- Section 126: Licensing of network facilities, network services, and applications services. Requires entities providing these services to hold either an individual or class licence.
- Section 105: Prohibition on the provision of content applications service without a licence. Applies to entities like broadcasting services or subscription TV platforms.
- Section 109: Limited content applications service exemptions. Allows for content services limited by scope, area, or audience to fall under class licences.

Part IX: Social Regulation
- Section 211: Prohibition on provision of offensive content. Mandates that all licensed content providers ensure their platforms avoid objectionable or harmful material.
- Section 212: Content Forum and Content Code. Establishes a framework for content guidelines and oversight, which licensees must comply with.

Part VI: Economic Regulation
- Section 129: Exemption for applications service providers not subject to a class licence. Applications service provider not subject to a class licence. Provides exemptions for certain applications services (e.g., web hosting, certain internet services) based on specific criteria.
- Section 130: Nominated facilities provider. Allows the Minister to designate a licensed network facilities provider as a nominated facilities provider, which may have implications for licensing.
- Section 131: Providers under a class licence shall register. Requires that any person operating under a class licence must register with the Commission.

Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission MCMC Licensing Guidebook 2023
Section 2.1(a) Eligibility of Persons Applying for Licence outlines that the following persons or classes of persons shall be ineligible to apply for an individual licence: a) A foreign company as defined under the Companies Act 2016.

Individual Licence:
- Section 2.1 Eligibility of Persons Applying for Individual Licence. Specifies who is eligible to apply for an individual licence, including the requirement for Bumiputera shareholding. Individual Licence is detailed in Section 3:
Applications require:
	• Paid-up capital of RM2,000,000 for network facilities/service licences.
	• Paid-up capital of RM500,000 for content applications services licences.
	• Minimum Bumiputera shareholding of 30%.
	• Application fee: RM10,000 per licence.

Malaysia.coverage: Internet Messaging and Social Media Service Providers
Malaysia.impact:
The Information Paper on Regulatory Framework for Internet Messaging and Social Media Service Providers (August 2024) outlines specific licensing requirements for various online service providers under the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 (CMA 1998). Key sections relevant to social media platforms, VPNs, and cloud services include:

- Section 4.1: Social media service providers with over 8 million users in Malaysia must apply for an Application Service Provider Class Licence (ASP (C)) under the CMA 1998.
- Section 4.7: The paper explicitly defines social media services as applications enabling users to create, upload, share, or modify content. This applies to a broad range of services, including VPNs and cloud service providers.
- Section 4.13: Cloud service providers are regulated under a similar licensing framework, following a “light-touch” regulatory approach that allows innovation while maintaining oversight.
- Section 4.17: Licensed providers must comply with obligations under Section 263 of the CMA 1998, ensuring that they prevent their services from being used in illegal activities and provide assistance to law enforcement.
-Section 4.21: The validity period for the class licence is one (1) year from the registration date and the Service Providers need to re-apply every year provided they meet the threshold of having eight (8) million or more users in Malaysia.

These sections demonstrate that social media platforms, VPNs, and cloud services are required to be licensed, and failure to do so by January 1, 2025, will result in penalties under Section 126 of the Communications and Multimedia Act.

The Standards of Malaysia Act 1996 (Act 549) stipulates that the Department of Standards Malaysia (DSM) will function as the National Standards Body (NSB) as well as the National Accreditation Body (NAB). The national standardization system uses a consensus process to develop new standards.

Malaysia.impact:
SIRIM QAS International remains Malaysia’s leading conformity assessment body, providing services such as testing, inspection, and certification across multiple sectors. However, it is no longer the sole certifying agency in all contexts. While SIRIM is a key player for product certification and standards, other accredited bodies also operate in Malaysia, depending on the industry and certification type.

Type Approval is a mandatory requirement for a communications product before it is allowed to be sold in Malaysia. Among the products that need to be certified there are: netbook, tablet PC, mobile phone, security devices, smart TV, WiFi/3G/Bluetooth module and broadcasting equipment. Foreign applicants shall appoint a local management representative in Malaysia to be responsible for Type Approval application. As a member of the Asia Pacific Economic Cooperation Mutual Recognition Arrangement (APEC-MRA), Malaysia accepts product approvals from APEC members.

Self-certification is not allowed under MCMC guidelines. 
Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 (CMA 1998):
- Section 186 empowers the MCMC to register certifying agencies to ensure compliance with codes or standards. This mandates that all radio and telecommunication equipment meet technical specifications certified by SIRIM QAS or equivalent agencies. “The Commission may register certifying agencies or classes of certifying agencies, including agencies outside Malaysia, for the purposes of certifying compliance with codes or standards under this Part.”
- Section 185 requires compliance with technical standards to ensure the safe operation of equipment that utilizes the radio spectrum or connects to public telecommunications networks.

Communications and Multimedia (Technical Standards) Regulations 2000:
- Regulation 14 mandates that all radio frequency (RF) and EMC-emitting devices be certified by the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) through accredited testing facilities like SIRIM QAS. “No person shall manufacture, import, or distribute communication equipment unless such equipment has been approved in accordance with these regulations.”

Electricity Regulations 1994 (Under the Electricity Supply Act 1990):
- Regulation 97 ensures that all electrical equipment, are tested for compliance before they are sold or distributed. Testing must be conducted in authorized laboratories.

Malaysia.act: Standards of Malaysia Act (Act 549) 1996

The process of self-certification is limited and requires oversight from accredited 3rd party bodies (Standards of Malaysia Act (Act 549) 1996). The Act mandates that a conformity assessment body accredited by the Department of Standards, Malaysia must be involved in product certification to ensure that products meet the required safety and performance standards. Self-certification is generally not permitted for products that fall under regulated standards.

Section 16(1) of the Act specifies that any person desiring to certify products must apply for accreditation as a conformity assessment body. This effectively limits the ability of manufacturers or importers to self-certify their products. Any false representation of conformity assessment (such as misleading consumers into believing that a product complies with standards when it does not) is subject to significant penalties, including fines and imprisonment.

Malaysia.impact:
There is no limits on foreign ownership on e-commerce in Malaysia. 

Malaysia Electronic Commerce Act (Act 658) 2006 does not impose specific foreign equity limits on the e-commerce sector. Unlike regulated industries like telecommunications or financial services, which have explicit foreign ownership caps (e.g., 30% for broadcasting or specific caps in financial services), the e-commerce sector is generally open to foreign ownership. However, foreign investors must comply with general corporate laws and tax regulations.

Malaysia.act_norm: guideline on foreign participation in the distributive trade services malaysia 2020
acts_norm: guideline on foreign participation in the distributive trade services malaysia 2020
Malaysia.impact:
The Guideline on Foreign Participation in the Distributive Trade Services Malaysia 2020: If an e-commerce business involves, sectors such as logistics, telecommunications, or digital payments, foreign equity restrictions applicable to those sectors may apply. For instance, logistics operations may fall under distributive trade regulations, which can impose some limitations on foreign equity. In distributive trade, foreign equity is limited to 70% for hypermarkets, and smaller retail formats, like convenience stores, may have stricter 30% caps (Section 6.2.3). While logistics is not directly categorized under retail or wholesale, if a logistics company is involved in distributive trade activities (e.g., warehousing, delivery of retail goods), it may need to comply with these sectoral restrictions. 

Specifically, hypermarket means a self-service distribution store with a sales floor area of 5,000 square meters or more, selling a wide variety of mainly consumer goods, comprising a combination of food and non-food products and in different forms of packaging (Section 6.1).

For example, businesses must comply with the Companies Act 2016 for incorporation, and foreign-owned entities must adhere to specific capital and incorporation requirements as outlined in the Distributive Trade Guidelines.

The Guideline on Foreign Participation in the Distributive Trade Services Malaysia 2020: For foreign businesses involved in e-commerce, the following requirements apply:

Traditional Licensing and Approvals: E-commerce businesses may need approval from the Ministry of Domestic Trade and Consumer Affairs (MDTCA) for foreign-owned distributive trade operations.

The Guidelines on Foreign Participation in Distributive Trade Services in Malaysia published by Ministry of Domestic Trade and Consumer Affairs 2020 does not provide a specific licensing scheme exclusively for e-commerce providers, it indicates that e-commerce businesses involved in distributive trade must comply with the same licensing and approval processes as traditional retail businesses. The licensing requirements applicable to traditional business are:

- Minimum Capital Requirements: Depending on the type of distributive trade, there are minimum capital requirements that must be met. For example: Hypermarkets require a minimum capital investment of RM50 million, Departmental stores require RM20 million, other distributive trade activities may have different thresholds, often starting from RM1 million.

acts_norm: bank negara malaysia policy document on electronic money 2022
Malaysia.impact:
For e-commerce and online payments, businesses handling payments need to adhere to standards issued by the Bank Negara Malaysia (BNM). Under the Malaysian Financial Services Act (Act 758) 2013 (FSA), businesses handling e-commerce payments and digital transactions must adhere to standards issued by Bank Negara Malaysia (BNM). Some relevant standards include:

- Approval for Designated Payment Instruments (Section 11): Issuers of e-money and payment systems must obtain approval from BNM before providing these services. This ensures compliance with security and reliability standards for digital transactions.
- Electronic Money (E-Money) Guidelines: Issuers of e-wallets and digital payment instruments must follow BNM’s policies for fund safeguarding, interoperability, and consumer protection. Examples of e-money include GrabPay, ShopeePay, and Touch ’n Go eWallet must ensure Fund Safeguarding, Transaction Limits and Dispute Resolution.
- Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI DSS): Payment service providers are required to comply with PCI DSS to protect cardholder data during e-commerce transactions. This includes encryption, fraud detection, and secure payment environments. Secure Payment Processing Requirements such as the use of multi-factor authentication (e.g., One-Time Passwords or OTPs) for customer verification, implementation of fraud detection and monitoring systems to identify and mitigate suspicious activities in real-time and compliance with strong encryption protocols like SSL/TLS to secure data transmissions
- Reporting and Monitoring: Providers of online payment systems are subject to periodic audits and must submit reports to BNM as per regulatory requirements to ensure operational transparency

The Bank Negara Malaysia (BNM) Policy Document on Electronic Money 2022 sets operational requirements for e-money issuers (EMIs), including:
- Capital Requirements: Non-bank EMIs must maintain a minimum capital of RM1 million or 8% of their outstanding liabilities, while eligible EMIs need RM5 million or 8%. Non-bank EMIs are required to maintain a minimum capital of RM1 million or 8% of their outstanding liabilities, whichever is higher. Eligible EMIs need RM5 million or 8%, as specified in Section 15.
- Trust Accounts: Funds collected in exchange for e-money must be held in trust accounts to ensure they are protected and cannot be misused

Malaysia.act:
Sales Tax (Amendment) Act (Act A1671) 2022; 

Customs (Prohibition of Imports) Order P.U. (A) 117 2023
Malaysia.act_norm: sales tax act 2022 customs order p u 117 2023
Malaysia.impact:
Malaysia’s de minimis rule exempts goods with a value of up to RM 500 (approximately USD 120 or 84 Special Drawing Rights) from customs duties and taxes under certain conditions. This rule is primarily aimed at simplifying customs procedures for low-value goods, particularly for postal and courier shipments. Below are the specific references and requirements related to this rule:

- Customs Exemptions: Goods with a Cost, Insurance, and Freight (CIF) value of RM 500 or less are exempted from import duties and sales tax. This exemption applies only to goods sent via air courier services and excludes road or sea transport.
- Low-Value Goods (LVG) Framework: The exemption was initially designed to facilitate small shipments and streamline customs processing. However, as of 2024, goods below RM 500 are subject to a 10% sales tax for low-value imports under the Sales Tax (Amendment) Act (Act A1671) 2022, aimed at creating parity between online and offline businesses. The sales tax is charged on the sale value of LVG not including any tax, duty, fee or other charges such as transportation, insurance or other costs.
Section 11a: Defines "low value goods" and outlines the imposition of sales tax.
Section 11b: States that the provisions of the Act apply to low-value goods.
Section 11c: Territorial and extra-territorial application of the Act.
- Exclusions from De Minimis: Items listed under the Customs (Prohibition of Imports) Order P.U. (A) 117 2023, excise goods, and certain regulated products do not qualify for this exemption, even if their value is below the threshold.

The new Implementations and Adjustments policies are part of Malaysia’s broader strategy to regulate e-commerce imports while maintaining a favorable environment for international trade.

Malaysia.act: World Trade Organization (WTO) Moratorium on Customs Duties on Electronic Transmissions 1998
Malaysia.act_norm: world trade organization moratorium on customs duties on electronic transmissions 1998
acts_norm: world trade organization moratorium on customs duties on electronic transmissions 1998
Malaysia.impact:
Malaysia and other economies  adhere to the World Trade Organization (WTO) Moratorium on Customs Duties on Electronic Transmissions, which was extended in 2022. This agreement means no customs duties are applied to the cross-border transmission of digital goods, including software, music, and e-books. The moratorium has been in place since 1998, and discussions regarding its renewal or potential modification was approved during the session of February 2024 at the WTO’s 13th Ministerial Conference.

Some developing countries, including Malaysia, have expressed concerns about potential revenue losses due to the inability to impose such duties. On the other hand, developed countries typically support the continuation of the moratorium, emphasizing its role in facilitating digital trade and ensuring market predictability.

While Malaysia does not currently impose customs duties on electronic transmissions, the possibility of ending the moratorium has sparked debate. If the moratorium is lifted, Malaysia and other nations might consider introducing tariffs on digital transmissions to generate additional revenue. However, the potential effects on consumers and businesses, particularly small and medium enterprises (SMEs), remain important factors in the ongoing discussions.

Under the World Trade Organization (WTO) framework, countries have agreed not to impose customs duties on electronic transmissions, including items like software downloads, music, films, and other digital content, to foster global digital trade. As of 25 June 2024, there are 91 WTO members participating in these discussions (Including Malaysia), accounting for over 90 per cent of global trade. In the absence of specific duties, Malaysia, along with other WTO members, follows the Joint Statement Initiative (JSI) which reinforces the moratorium on such customs duties for now. Nevertheless, countries may opt to implement taxes like VAT or GST on digital services instead of tariffs.

This includes providing a valid registration number and other supporting documents that confirm their physical presence in the country.

The official certificates emanates from the Companies Commission of Malaysia (Suruhanjaya Syarikat Malaysia), specifically related to the incorporation and name change of companies under the Companies Act 1965. The Borang 8 (Perakuan Perbadanan Syarikat Awam) and Borang 9 (Perakuan Pemerbadanan Syarikat Sendirian) outlines the registration requirements leading to the Borang D. Which is a Certificate of Registration under the Business Registration Act 1956 (Akta Pendaftaran Perniagaan 1956) from the Companies Commission of Malaysia (Suruhanjaya Syarikat Malaysia). This document, known as Form D, serves as official proof that a business has been registered in Malaysia.

As of September 2023, Malaysia has relaxed the physical presence requirement for registering a .MY domain. International entities can now register a .MY domain without having a local physical presence, provided they appoint a local administrative contact. This change allows foreign companies to more easily establish an online presence in Malaysia while still ensuring that they comply with local regulations through a representative within the country (A Representative Office or Regional Office ). This relaxation is part of the effort to make domain registration more accessible to global businesses while maintaining oversight through a local contact for administrative purposes.

Malaysia.act_norm: companies act 2016 communications and multimedia act 1998 information paper on regulatory framework for internet messaging and social media providers 2024
acts_norm: information paper on regulatory framework for internet messaging and social media providers 2024
Malaysia.impact:
Malaysia does have local presence requirements for online service providers under the Communications and Multimedia Act (Act 588) 1998 (CMA). Specifically, the Information Paper on Regulatory Framework for Internet Messaging and Social Media Providers 2024 outlines that online service providers, including social media platforms, must be locally incorporated to apply for the Application Service Provider Class Licence (ASP (C)). Additionally, while foreign ownership is allowed under the class licence, the service provider must establish a local entity in Malaysia to provide services and comply with Malaysian regulations.

Local presence requirements apply to certain types of online service providers, particularly cloud service providers (CSPs). Since January 1, 2022, CSPs providing services such as Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) or Platform as a Service (PaaS) are required to obtain an Applications Service Provider Class (ASP(C)) license from the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) if they have a local presence or use a local data center. This requirement does not apply to Software as a Service (SaaS) providers or resellers who do not control the cloud infrastructure directly.

Foreign CSPs without a local presence may still be required to obtain a license depending on their method of delivering services, especially if they operate through a local data center. However, companies that have no local presence and do not use local infrastructure are generally exempt from these licensing requirements, though this may limit their regulatory protection in Malaysia.

Malaysia.act_norm: electronic commerce act 2006
Malaysia.impact: Malaysia has established several legal frameworks to address online consumer protection, including the Electronic Commerce Act (Act 658) 2006 and the Consumer Protection Act (Act 599) 1999. These laws provide recognition for electronic transactions and ensure that consumers are protected in online dealings. The Electronic Commerce Act (Act 658) 2006 specifically addresses the legal recognition of electronic messages and transactions in commercial dealings, ensuring that online contracts and transactions are legally enforceable.

Consumer Protection Act (Act 599) 1999
Malaysia.act_norm: consumer protection regulations 2012 consumer protection act 1999
Malaysia.impact:
The Consumer Protection (Electronic Trade Transactions) Regulations 2012, made under the Consumer Protection Act 1999, provide a legal framework for online consumer protection in Malaysia. Key sections include:
- Disclosure of Information (Regulation 3): Businesses must disclose critical information such as the name of the business, registration number, contact details, and a full description of goods or services, including their price and associated costs. Failure to do so constitutes an offense.
- Rectification of Errors and Acknowledgment of Receipt (Regulation 4): Online businesses must provide a way for buyers to correct any errors before confirming their order and acknowledge receipt of the order without undue delay.
- Record Maintenance (Regulation 5): Online marketplace operators must maintain a record of sellers’ names, contact details, and addresses for at least two years

Singapore.act_norm: countervailing and anti dumping duties act 1996
acts_norm: countervailing and anti dumping duties act 1996
Singapore.impact: This act allows the government to investigate and impose duties when there is evidence of unfair trade practices, such as dumping or subsidies causing injury to domestic industries. There are no anti-dumping, countervailing duty or safeguard measures against ICT products.
Singapore.timeframe: Since November 1996 last amended in December 2021

Singapore.act_norm: government procurement act 1997 government procurement regulations 2014 government procurement order 2002
Singapore.impact:
The Government Procurement Act enforces the provisions of the World Trade Organization Agreement on Government Procurement and other international obligations related to public procurement. Section 6 of the Act states that the Minister may for the purposes of implementing the Agreement on Government Procurement and other international obligations of Singapore relating to procurements by Government and public authorities, make regulations to govern procurements subject to the Act. 

Foreign suppliers are granted access to Singapore's public procurement market. The Government Procurement Regulations govern procurements that fall under the Act, outlining the types of procedures that can be used during procurement processes (Part II Procedure leading to award of procurement contract, and Part III Procedure on award of procurement contract). The regulations ensure non-discriminatory access for foreign suppliers, they permit limited tendering or selective tendering in certain circumstances. The Government Procurement (Application) Order identifies the states, authorities, and types of procurement subject to the Act, along with those exempted from its application.  

Both the GP Regulations and GP Order are subsidiary legislation under the Act.

Singapore.act_norm: government procurement act 1997 government procurement regulations 2014 government procurement regulation 2002 government procurement order 2002
Singapore.impact:
Singapore does not implement to use certain type of encryption, source code and trade secrets to win tenders. 

Government Procurement Regulations 6(b): Requires mechanisms that ensure the integrity of requests for participation and tenders, including preventing inappropriate access to sensitive information such as trade secrets.

Government Procurement (Application) Order Paragraph 5(3)(b) allows for exclusions related to intellectual property, which could include protections for trade secrets or sensitive technologies like source code in ICT products.

All procurement requirements, procedures, and evaluation criteria for bids and tenders from a contracting authority must be disclosed to suppliers. According to Regulation 10 of the Government Procurement Regulations, for each procurement, the contracting authority is required to publish a notice of intended procurement on the GeBIZ platform or in the Government Gazette. Additionally, specific rules apply to electronic auctions.

Singapore.act_norm: government procurement act 1997 government procurement regulations 2014 government procurement order 2002
Singapore.impact:
The principles of national treatment and non-discrimination are set out in the Government Procurement Regulations 2014. For instance, an applicable supplier is not to be treated less favourably than any other applicable supplier (Section 4). Most of Singapore's international trade agreements contain Government Procurement chapters, including provisions on national treatment and non-discrimination. According to the authorities, open sourcing and non-discrimination are also the default practices for procurements that are not covered by Singapore's trade agreements.

Government Procurement Act Section 6: Allows the Minister to make regulations regarding government procurement, including setting limitations on foreign participation where national interests or security concerns arise.
A contracting authority is prohibited from using limited tendering to avoid competition or to favor suppliers based in Singapore, or in any way that discriminates against eligible suppliers. If a contracting authority is suspected of violating the competitive bidding rules, a supplier or bidder can file a challenge with the Government Procurement Adjudication Tribunal ("Tribunal") for the alleged breach of duty by the authority.

There is no ownership restriction for sectors relevant to digital trade, for example, manufacturing of information and communication technology goods and products, computer and related services, and online broadcasting. 

The Companies Act 1967 of Singapore does not specifically limit foreign equity ownership in companies incorporated in Singapore.

Joint ventures can be entered between two companies under the Singapore Companies Act 1967, chapter 50, Section 215AA. Singapore does not mandate joint venture requirement for companies to invest in the country.

Section 215AA of the Companies Act 1967 relates to an offer to acquire all the shares in a company, or all of the shares in any particular class in a company, by 2 or more persons jointly, and does not pertain to whether joint ventures can be entered into between two companies. There are no specific requirements under the Companies Act on a foreign company entering into a joint venture with a local company.

Singapore.impact: Pursuant to the Singapore Companies Act 1967 (section 145(1)), a director must be a natural person (i.e. a company or business entity cannot be a director) , at least one director must be ordinarily resident in Singapore—that is, Singapore citizen, Singapore permanent resident, EntrePass holder, or Employment Pass (EP) holder. A company is required to have at least one director who is ordinarily resident in Singapore. It must also have a company secretary, who cannot be a sole director and must be resident in Singapore.

Singapore.act_norm: telecommunications act 1999 telecom and mediacompetition code 2012
Singapore.impact: Low thresholds for merger and acquisitions in the telecommunication sector are in place in Singapore. According to section 10 of the Telecommunications Competition Code and pursuant to section 26 of the Telecommunications Act, acquisitions higher than 12% of a controller of a designated telecommunications licensee must seek prior approval from the Infocomm Media Development Authority of Singapore (IMDA). Likewise, in the case of acquisitions higher than 30% of the total voting shares or voting power which results in a 30% or higher controlling stake of the designated telecommunication licensee, prior approval of the IMDA is required.

Since April 2012

Singapore.impact: In Significant Investments Review Act 2024, Section 18 of the act requires investors to provide written notification to the government before acquiring a 5 percent stake, and Section 19 obliges those obtaining any stake over 12 percent in these entities to receive prior approval. Existing investors with shares over 50 percent in these entities must also receive approval before divesting their stakes. Section 19 outlines that a person must obtain prior written approval from the Minister to acquire significant equity interests or control over voting power in a designated entity. Section 32 empowers the Minister to review and issue directions if a significant investment or acquisition of equity interests is deemed to pose a threat to Singapore's national security.  Section 34 stipulates that the Minister or authorized officers have the power to gather information related to any equity interests or control of voting power in designated entities to ensure compliance with national security interests.

Under section 368, every foreign company must establish a place of business in Singapore before carry on business in the country. A foreign company shall have a registered office in Singapore to which all communications and notices may be addressed and which shall be open and accessible to the public for not less than 5 hours between 9 a.m. and  5 p.m. each business day (Section 370).

Singapore.act_norm: patents act 1994 patent act 2017 patent rules
Singapore.impact:
Section 34(1) of Patents Act 1994 imposes a mandatory pre-filing requirement on persons resident in Singapore: they must either first file domestically (at least two months prior) and receive no pending secrecy direction, or obtain written authority from the Registrar before filing abroad. 

Besides, according to Rule 23(1A), using obligatory language "shall be in English" — on all applicants for all documents making up a patent application. The requirement applies universally to all filers (domestic and foreign) and constitutes a procedural burden on filing eligibility, as non-English documents must be translated into English before or at filing.

There are civil and administrative procedures and remedies, and provisional measures in Singapore. Part XIII of Singapore’s Patents Act 1994 addresses patent infringement claims within Singapore. Such claims must be filed in the General Division of the High Court. Previously, until 1 April 2022, the Intellectual Property Office of Singapore (IPOS) was also authorized to handle patent infringement matters if both parties agreed to refer the dispute to IPOS. However, due to an amendment to the Patents Act, since 1 April 2022, only the General Division of the High Court can adjudicate patent infringement claims, unless there is an alternative agreement, such as one to arbitrate. Proprietors, co-proprietors and exclusive licensees of patents have locus standi to sue for patent infringement.
Singapore.timeframe:
Since February 1995 last amended in November 2022;

Since April 2017

Singapore.impact: No restriction on the enforcement of patents. Civil proceedings may be brought in the court by the proprietor of a patent in respect of any act alleged to infringe the patent (Section 67). International patents enjoy the same protection as locally filed patents. The holders of international patent can take legal action in Singapore to enforce their rights and seek remedies like injunctions or damages for infringement (Section 67).
Singapore.timeframe:
Since February 1995 last amended in November 2022;

Since April 2017

Singapore.impact:
The Copyright Act 2021 provides the legal framework for copyright and contains provisions on copyright exceptions, such as fair use (Part 5 Division 2) and other permissible uses aligned with Art 9(2) of Berne Convention. 

The Copyright Act (Part 5 Division 2) recognises the fair dealing of copyright material for research and study.

Fair dealing only apply to specific purposes, such as: Research and private study, Criticism and review, News reporting, Parody and satire, Judicial proceedings or professional legal advice.

Singapore.act_norm: copyright act 2021 copyright regulation 2021 rules of court 2021 supreme court of judicature rule 2022 ip court guide copyright tribunals regulation 2021
Singapore.impact:
Copyright infringement proceedings in Singapore follow a process similar to standard court litigation. Under part 3, division 9 of Copyright Act 2021, copyright infringement proceedings in Singapore begin with a writ of summons and statement of claim. The defendant can then file a defense and counterclaim, with the claimant responding accordingly. The court may grant summary judgment if the defendant lacks a defense; otherwise, the case proceeds to trial. Key stages include discovery, filing affidavits of evidence, and presenting witnesses. Interlocutory applications are allowed throughout, and a judge, not a jury, decides the outcome.

Copyright infringement proceedings follow the process set out in Copyright Regulation 2021, Rules of Court 2021, Supreme Court of Judicature (Intellectual Property) Rule 2022, and the IP Court Guide. In the case of a copyright dispute relating to licences, licence schemes, royalties, equitable remuneration and the terms on which the Singapore Government can use copyright material, the process  is set out in Copyright Tribunals (Procedure) Regulation 2021. Remedies and Provisional Measures are set out in Copyright Act 2021.

An action against a person for an infringement of copyright may be brought in the Court by copyright owner or exclusive licensee 6 years after the infringement takes place (Sections 153 and 154). In Subdivision (3) - Section 325(1) of the Copyright Act allows the Court to order a Network Connection Provider (NCP) to disable access to an online location if (b)the online location has been or is being used to commit rights infringements in relation to copyright works or protected performances of which the applicant is the rights owner.

Section 315 restricts the remedies available against network service providers (NSPs) in case of copyright infringements, provided certain conditions are met. The court can order the NSP to disable access to infringing copies but not grant monetary remedies​. Sections 317 and 319 outline provisions for take-down notices, enabling rights owners to require NSPs to remove or disable access to infringing content on their primary networks. Section 324 establishes penalties for submitting false statements in take-down or restoration notices, ensuring that such measures are not misused​.

Confidential Information which comprises of copyrighted material (e.g. instruction manuals, computer software, databases) will be protected by both the law of confidential information under common law doctrine and the Copyright Act (chapter 63). The law on the protection of confidential information protects ideas and information not in the public domain, including trade secrets. This law is not codified in Singapore and is generally contractually controlled.

There is no mandatory disclosure of trade secrets.

Singapore.act:
Trade Secrets Enterprise Guide issued by Intellectual Property Office of Singapore;

Common Law doctrine of breach of confidence
Singapore.act_norm: trade secrets enterprise guide issued by intellectual property office of singapore common law doctrine of breach of confidence
acts_norm: trade secrets enterprise guide issued by intellectual property office of singapore
common law doctrine of breach of confidence
Singapore.impact:
In Singapore, there is no express legislation for the protection of trade secrets per se. Instead, protection of trade secrets in Singapore is typically through the common law doctrine of breach of confidence, as well as (where applicable) the use of IP and contract law. It protects all types of confidential information, such as your personal/private information (e.g., medical reports, photographs, receipts).

For information to be protectable as confidential information or a trade secret, it must not already be available to the public at large and it must be clearly and specifically identified (Nanofilm Technologies International Pte Ltd v Semivac International Pte Ltd and others [2018] SGHC 167). 

Additionally, the Intellectual Property Office of Singapore's Trade Secrets Enterprise Guide reports: In considering whether a breach of confidence is made out, the following will be considered: 1) whether the information has the quality of confidentiality; 2) whether the information was imparted in circumstances importing an obligation of confidentiality. An obligation of confidentiality can be found even where confidential information has been accessed or acquired without the company’s knowledge or consent; 3) whether the person who came into the information is able to demonstrate that his conscience was unaffected.  For instance that he was not aware that the information was confidential or he came across the information by accident.

Confidential information is protected by the common law doctrine – law of confidence, as well as contract law and various legislation(s) dependent on the circumstance. 

• For example, section 3 of the Computer Misuse Act 1993 may apply in cases where a person breaches cybersecurity in order to gain access to trade secrets whilst section 24 of the Personal Data Protection Act obligates the organisation collecting personal data to protect the personal data by making reasonable security arrangements. 
The Trade Secrets Enterprise Guide issued by the Intellectual Property Office of Singapore provides further guidance on this.

The Computer Misuse Act 1993 (“CMA”) may also apply in cases where your cybersecurity has been breached by a person seeking to obtain your trade secrets. Under the CMA, it is an offence for persons to knowingly cause a computer to perform a function in order to gain unauthorized access to any programme or data held in any computer (section 3(1) of the CMA).
Singapore.timeframe: Since August 1993 last amended in December 2021

Code of Practice for Competition in the Provision of Telecommunication and Media Services 2022 issued by Infocomm Media Development Authority (IMDA)
Singapore.act_norm: telecommunications act 1999 code of practice for competition in the provision of telecommunication and media services 2022 issued by infocomm media development authority
acts_norm: code of practice for competition in the provision of telecommunication and media services 2022 issued by infocomm media development authority
Singapore.impact:
In Telecommunication Act 1999, Section 26 prohibits exclusive agreements between developers, landowners, or occupiers and telecommunication providers that would restrict access to infrastructure by other licensees, ensuring that telecommunication facilities can be shared among different service providers.

Under the Code of Practice for Competition, IMDA will require sharing of any Telecommunication Infrastructure or Media Resource that it determines is Critical Support Infrastructure or an Essential Resource (Section 7). The Critical Support Infrastructure or an Essential Resource is based on the criteria, such as when telecommunication infrastructure is not fully utilised, and an new entrant would neither be able to replicate the telecommunication infrastructure (Section 7.3.1).

Since 2022

Singapore.impact:
The Singapore telecoms sector was fully liberalised on 1 April 2000. Since then, there have been no direct or indirect foreign equity limits imposed on persons holding a licence to provide telecom services in both fixed and mobile services.

Singapore allows up to 100% foreign ownership in facilities-based operators (FBOs) that provide telecommunication services, including mobile and broadband services. The Telecommunications Act 1999, under Section 38 (Part 5A), provides IMDA with oversight powers concerning significant changes in equity interests, voting power, or control in telecommunication licensees. Section 38 requires approval from IMDA if an investor intends to acquire significant control (e.g., more than 5%, 12%, or 30% of voting shares) in designated telecommunication licensees.

acts_norm: telecommunications limited owned by temasek holdings
Singapore.impact:
Temasek Holdings is Singapore’s sovereign wealth fund, fully owned by the government, and it holds significant shares in Singapore Telecommunications Limited (Singtel), one of the largest telecommunications operators in Singapore and the region.
Singtel is the largest telecommunications company in Singapore and one of the largest in Asia.  As of 31 March 2024, Temasek Holdings owns approximately 51% of Singtel, making it the major shareholder. This level of government ownership provides Singapore with strategic control over one of its key national telecommunications assets. Tamasek also owns 100% equity shares in Singapore Technologies Telemedia Pte Ltd (ST Telemedia), which is an investment company that owns shares in another telecom incumbent,  StarHub.

Singapore.impact:
Accounting separation is required for the application of dominant licensees and their related companies. Under section 2.3 of the Converged Code, a licensee will be classified as ‘dominant’ if it is licensed to operate facilities that are sufficiently costly or difficult to replicate such that requiring new entrants to do so would create a significant barrier to rapid and successful entry into the telecommunication and (or) media market in Singapore by an efficient competitor; or if it has the ability to exercise significant market power in any market in Singapore in which it provides telecommunication or broadcasting services. 

The Telecom Competition Code Section 8.1.4.1.3 requires these operators to separate their financial accounts for different services to prevent cross-subsidization and to enhance cost transparency. Accounting separation involves the preparation of separate financial statements for different segments of the business, such as wholesale and retail operations.

Accounting separation is a tool available to the regulator, but are not applied by default.Section 8.1.4.1.3 of the Telecom and Media Competition Code prohibits a Telecommunication Licensee with significant market power from abusing its dominant position by engaging in cross-subsidisation. It does result in a default requirement for operators to separate their financial accounts for different services. A Telecommunication Licensee found to have contravened Section 8.1.4.1.3 of the Telecom and Media Competition Code will be subject to remedial actions by the IMDA, and accounting separation is one of such actions that IMDA can consider imposing.

Accounting Separation Guidelines has been enacted.

Singapore does not mandate functional separation (the operational division between retail and wholesale units) for SMP operators.

Singapore.impact:
According to Section 26, an organisation may only transfer personal data outside Singapore if it has taken appropriate steps to ensure that: 
- it will comply with the Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) obligations in respect of the transferred personal data while it remains in its possession or under its control; and 
- the recipient outside of Singapore is bound by legally enforceable obligations to provide a standard of protection to the personal data transferred that is comparable to that under the PDPA. 

In this regard, “legally enforceable obligations” would include obligations imposed on the recipient: (i) under law (e.g. the recipient’s relevant national data privacy legislation); (ii) under any contract: (a) requiring the recipient to provide a standard of protection to the personal data transferred that is at least comparable to the protection under the PDPA; and (b) specifying the countries and territories to which the personal data may be transferred under the contract; (iii) under binding corporate rules (see below); or (iv) any other legally binding instrument.

An organisation will be taken to have satisfied the second requirement if the individual consents to the transfer of the personal data to the recipient in that country.

Singapore.act_norm: personal data protection act 2012 personal data protection act 2020
Singapore.impact:
The Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (PDPA) provides a baseline standard of protection for personal data in Singapore. It complements sector-specific legislative and regulatory frameworks such as the Banking Act and Insurance Act, Healthcare Services Act, Telecommunications Act, etc. The PDPA comprises various requirements governing the collection, use, disclosure and care of personal data in Singapore. Personal Data Protection Commission (PDPC) administers and enforces the PDPA.

Moreover, the PDPA amendment in 2021 introduces a number of key reforms, including mandatory data breach notification requirements, amendments to the consent obligation, offences for egregious mishandling of personal data, prohibitions relating to the use of dictionary attacks and address-harvesting software, and the PDPC's power to accept voluntary undertakings as part of its enforcement regime.

Since February 2021

Singapore.impact:
Under Section 47 of the Banking Act 1970 (s47 BA), banks and their officers are responsible for safeguarding the confidentiality of customer information. A bank or any of its officers may not disclose customer information to any other person, except where specified under the Third Schedule, e.g. where the bank has obtained written consent from the customer to disclose the information. 
Section 47 BA also stipulates penalties for breaching these confidentiality requirements.

Cybersecurity Act 2018 establishes a legal framework for the oversight and maintenance of national cybersecurity in Singapore. The key objectives of the Cybersecurity Act are to: (i) strengthen the protection of Critical Information Infrastructure against cyber-attacks; (ii) authorize the government to prevent and respond to cybersecurity threats and incidents; (iii); establish a framework for sharing cybersecurity information, and (iv) establish a licensing framework for cybersecurity service providers. Amendments to the Act were made in May 2024 to update the Act so that it keeps pace with the developments in the cyber threat landscape, as well as our evolving technological operating context. The key amendments: (i) updated existing provisions relating to cybersecurity of Critical Information Infrastructure, and (ii) expanded CSA’s oversight to cover new classes of regulated entities.

Singapore.impact:
Under Section 11(3), each organisation is required to appoint one or more data protection officers to be responsible to ensure the organisation’s compliance with the Personal Data Protection Act. Moreover, data protection impact assessment (“DPIA”) guides has been implemented. 

Organisations are also advised to conduct data protection impact assessments before releasing products or services that are likely to be accessed by children in order to meet their Accountability Obligation under the PDPA.

Under the Criminal Procedure Code, (section 39 and 40) police officers have the power to access computers and the power to access decrypted information. Moreover, (section 20) empowers the police to order the "production of any document or other thing" for the purposes of criminal investigations. Any person who obstructs the lawful exercise by a police officer or an authorised person shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding $10,000 or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 3 years or to both.

The Part 6 Division 2 of Copyright Act 2021 creates a “safe-harbour + notice-and-takedown” regime for network service providers (NSPs) and network connection providers (NCPs) in Singapore. The purposes are to Shield providers from monetary liability (damages, profits, statutory damages) when their networks are used for copyright infringement, provided they meet the statutory conditions, and
limit available court remedies to narrowly defined, non-monetary orders—mainly disabling access, removing or restoring content, or terminating specific accounts.

Four protected technical activities include Transmission, routing or providing connections (§316), System caching (§317), Storage at a user’s direction (§318), Referring or linking via search engines, hyperlinks, etc. (§319)
For each activity, the statute lists precise conditions (automatic processes, no editorial control, no direct financial benefit from infringement, expeditious compliance with takedown notices, designation of a notice agent, etc.).

Providers who act in good faith to remove or disable access are immunised from liability, even if the content is later found non-infringing.
Affected users may file a “restoration notice”; the provider must restore the material unless the rights owner commences court action within 10 working days.
There is no obligation to monitor networks proactively.
Safe-harbour eligibility is rebuttably presumed if prescribed evidence is produced.
Making a false statement in any notice is an offence (up to S$10,000 or 2 years’ imprisonment) and exposes the sender to damages.
Overall, the Division codifies a balanced framework: providers that play a passive, technical role obtain strong liability protection, while rights owners retain a quick, low-cost mechanism to address apparent infringements.

Singapore.act_norm: protection from online falsehoods and manipulation act 2019
acts_norm: protection from online falsehoods and manipulation act 2019
Singapore.impact: In both intent and practice, the Protection from Online Falsehoods and Manipulation Act (POFMA) relies primarily on Correction Directions to dispel falsehoods. Correction Directions require facts to be juxtaposed against falsehoods, which are not subject to removal or restricted access. Members of the public can read both the facts and the falsehoods and make up their own minds. Access blocking measures under POFMA are only intended to be used against the most egregious of cases (e.g. malicious deepfakes). As of 31 May 2025, no Disabling Direction under POFMA has been issued. Section 28(4) of POFMA does not impose liability on internet intermediaries. Rather, it provides a safe harbour for internet access service providers in relation to access blocking orders. The safe harbour provisions for internet intermediaries should also include Sections 15(3), 16(4), 27(4), 33(5), 34(6), 39(2), 42(3), 43(4), 51(2) and 54(5) of POFMA.

Under the Online Safety (Miscellaneous Amendments) Act (since November 2022, into effect in February 2023), if the person provides an online communication service and the person provides internet access service with a Singapore end-user link resonably know that the content is "egregious content" and fail to disable access will be subject to fine or outright blocking in Singapore (Sections 45E, 45F, and 45I).

In other words, OCSs and providers of an internet access service (under s45F) have to comply with any direction issued by the Authority under s45H (to disable access to content/stop communication) or s45I (to block access to the OCS) respectively to avoid criminal liability, and they are not required to monitor content under those sections.  And Section 45I  provides the Authority the power to direct the provider of the internet access service to stop access by Singapore end-users to the online communication service.

code of practice for e commerce services
Singapore.impact:
Under the Online Criminal Harms Act, the Competent Authority can issue Codes of Practice to require providers of designated online services to put in place appropriate systems, processes or measures to proactively disrupt scams and malicious cyber activities affecting people in Singapore. 

Under the Code of Practice for Online Communication Services, designated online services must implement appropriate systems, processes or measures to disrupt malicious accounts and activities quickly and prevent the propagation of malicious activities on their services. For instance, designated online services are required to proactively detect and take necessary action(s) against suspected scams and malicious cyber activities.  

Under the Code of Practice for E-Commerce Services, designated online services are required to implement the systems, processes and measures in the Code of Practice for Online Communication Services, and additional requirements including the verification of users against Government-issued records.

Singapore.impact: The Code of Practice for Online Safety – Social Media Services, enacted under the Broadcasting Act aims to enhance online user safety, particularly for children, and curb the spread of harmful content on designated Social Media Services (“Services”). According to the Code, designated Services shall (a) put in place systems and processes to minimise end-users’ exposure to harmful content, (b) empower end-users with safety tools and effective mechanisms to report harmful content, and (c) be accountable to Singapore end-users by submitting annual online safety reports with information on their safety measures, supported by suitable data that reflects the impact of their safety efforts in Singapore. For instance, designated Services shall use technologies and processes to proactively detect and swiftly remove child sexual exploitation and abuse material and terrorism content (Clause 15). Designated Services are also required to take reasonable and proportionate steps to proactively detect and swiftly remove preparatory child sexual exploitation and abuse activity and terrorism activity (Clause 16). In addition, all end-users, including children, should be protected from harmful content, such as sexual content, violent content, suicide and self-harm content, and cyberbullying content.”

Singapore.act_norm: broadcasting act 1994 internet code of practice
Singapore.impact: The Internet Code of Practice (“Code”) enacted under the Broadcasting Act outlines the content standards for Internet content. According to the Code, all Internet Service Providers (ISPs) and Internet Content Providers (ICPs) licensed under the Broadcasting Act, specifically the Broadcasting (Class Licence) Notification, shall use his best efforts to ensure that prohibited material is not broadcast via the Internet to users in Singapore (Clause 2). Specifically, the prohibited material is material that is objectionable on the grounds of public interest, public morality, public order, public security, national harmony, or is otherwise prohibited by applicable Singapore laws. In considering what is prohibited material, the following factors should be taken into account, for example, whether the material depicts nudity or genitalia in a manner calculated to titillate, whether the material promotes sexual violence or sexual activity involving coercion or non-consent of any kind and others, etc. (Clause 4).

In 2013, the then-Media Development Authority of Singapore issued guidelines (Online News Licensing Scheme framework) providing that all online news websites that provide regular reports on Singapore and have significant reach should acquire an individual licence if they:

Singapore.impact:
Singapore Customs maintains a licence number database for import of optical disc mastering and replication equipment for use in Singapore. Manufacturers of optical disc mastering and replication equipment must apply for a licence under the Manufacture of Optical Discs Act from the Singapore Economic Development Board (EDB). Only approved manufacturers who have been issued a "Manufacturer Number" by EDB will be allowed to import such goods for use in Singapore. An application for a licence is usually granted by EDB if it meets the stipulated criteria.
The licensing approach applies to products originating in and imported from all countries.
The licensing approach is not intended to restrict the quantity or value of imports, but to prevent illegal trade of pirated goods.
The licensing approach applies to mastering equipment and replication equipment for any of the following: CD (compact disc); CD-ROM (compact disc-read only memory); VCD (video compact disc); DVD (digital video disc); and DVD-ROM (digital video disc-read only memory).

The Singapore Accreditation Council (SAC) works closely with other international bodies on Mutual Recognition Arrangements (MRAs) which will allow signatories to mutually recognise reports and certificates issued by an accredited Conformity Assessment Body (CAB) as equivalent to their own standards. Moreover, Infocomm Media Development Authority (IMDA) accepts equipment certification by local or foreign certification bodies recognised by IMDA under a phase II mutual recognition arrangement.

Moreover, Singapore are parnter of the following MRAs: ASEAN MRA for Electronical and Electronic Equipment (ASEAN EE MRA), APEC MRA for Conformity Assessment of Telecommunications Equipment (APEC TEL MRA), and APEC Electronical and Electronic Equipment Mutual Recognition Arrangement (APEC EEMRA) (Part III Acceptance of certification). 

SDoC is only permitted in domestic businesses in Singapore. Equipment registration is based on the submission of the Supplier's Declaration of Conformity (SDoC). Enhanced Simplified Equipment Registration (ESER), Simplified Equipment Registration (SER), and General Equipment Registration (GER)—all of them require that the applicant be a local entity, specifically a local dealer registered with the Infocomm Media Development Authority (IMDA).

Singapore.coverage: Telecommunication sector, Information Technology, Wireless and Radio Equipment.
Singapore.impact:
Singapore's Infocomm Development Authority accepts equipment registration for many types of telecommunications and radio equipment for general sale and use in Singapore to be registered with IMDA. The purpose of equipment registration is to ensure that the telecommunication/radio-communication equipment is compatible with the public telecommunication network and does not cause radio frequency interference to other authoriszed radio-communication network/station, though registration is voluntary for some categories of equipment.  The company filing the registration must be a local company with a valid IMDA dealer's license. Registrations are typically based on foreign standard test reports/certifications to declare conformity to IMDA's technical standards. 

The IMDA accepts test reports, which present test results of equipment testing done according to IMDA’s Technical Specifications by (i) testing labs recognised by IMDA under MRA Phase I; (ii) testing labs accredited by accreditation bodies recognised by IMDA; or (iii) equipment manufacturers. IMDA also accepts equipment certification by local or foreign certification bodies recognised by IMDA under MRA Phase II. The Act on the Implementation of Mutual Recognition with Foreign Countries of the Results of Conformity Assessment Procedures for Specified Equipment (Chapter II, Articles 3-13) allows Singapore to recognise conformity assessment results from foreign laboratories under Mutual Recognition Agreements (MRAs).

Singapore has established various standards, and also align them with the international recognized encryption standards.

For instance, encryption methods, such as Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) has been adopted in quantum technologies. Moreover, Multi-Tier Cloud Security (MTCS), also known as Singapore Standard 584, is the world's first cloud security standard that covers multiple tiers. Prepared by the Information Technology Standards Committee (ITSC), MTCS defines how cloud service providers (CSPs) have to protect customer data and address their concerns about the confidentiality of the data in the cloud. With a total of 535 controls, it aims to provide transparency and visibility into how the CSPs handle data.

Singapore.impact: Under the Companies Act 1967, there is no foreign equity share limit. There is no foreign ownership in e-commerce sectors. Foreign entity is not prohibited from setting up a company in Singapore.
Singapore does not implement any restrictions on e-commerce purchases and delivery.
Singapore.timeframe: Since July 2010 last amended in December 2021

Singapore.impact: Internet Service/content providers, including e-commerce providers, based in Singapore are automatically class-licensed under the Broadcasting Act. According to Section 3, Internet service providers and some categories of content providers are required to register with the regulator (the IMDA) within 14 days of providing the service.

Under this Act, any person or entity conducting business in Singapore must register their business with the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority (ACRA) unless exempted. This requirement applies to both online and offline businesses.
Singapore.timeframe: Since 3 January 2016 last amended 26 in February 2024

Singapore.impact: Singaapore does not mandate local bank account for online paymeny.

Section 2 of Payment Services Act 2019 defines "e-money" and "digital payment tokens" without specifying a mandated currency. This allows businesses in Singapore the flexibility to transact in SGD or other currencies, especially for international services, as long as they comply with the requirements outlined in the PSA, such as anti-money laundering (AML) and counter-terrorism financing (CFT) obligations.

SGQR consolidates multiple payment QR codes into a single, unified code, simplifying e-payments for both consumers and merchants. This initiative, co-owned by the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) and the Infocomm Media Development Authority (IMDA), aims to promote interoperability among various payment schemes. This system is based on the widely used EMVCo QR code standard, and aligns with international standard.

Technical Reference (TR) 76 is a voluntary guidance standard developed by industry players, CASE, and government agencies to help new and existing e-commerce marketplaces and e-retailers build or improve on e-commerce processes and policies. It covers the end-to-end process of e-commerce transactions, from pre-purchase activities, to purchasing and payment procedures (including escrow payments), and to post-purchase activities. The guidelines also cover best practices on customer support and merchant verification. In 2022, it was revised to incorporate additional anti-scam measures, further strengthening protection for consumers transacting online.

Section 6(9)(a) of the PS Act states that the applicant has to be either a company; or a corporation formed or incorporated outside Singapore, where company refers to a company incorporated under the Companies Act 1967 in Singapore.
Section 6(9)(c) of the PS Act requires either of the two requirement to be fulfilled:
Executive director to be a citizen or permanent resident of Singapore
At least one director to be a citizen or permanent resident of Singapore, if the executive director is a Singapore Employment Pass holder, as prescribed in section 7 of PS Regulations.

According to section 24 of Payment Services Act 2019, amount that can be stored in personal payment account containing e-money: SGD $20,000 (or equivalent in foreign currency). Total amount transferred in any period of one-year from a personal payment account containing e-money: SGD $100,000 (or equivalent in foreign currency)
Singapore does not mandate the use of specific intermediaries for online transactions.

Licensed payment service provider cannot use customer money, or interest earned from customer money, to finance their business wholly or to any material extent. (Section 20). On section 18, licensee cannot use an agent to provide payment services, unless the agent has a licence or is an exempt payment service provider in respect of that type of payment service. Please refer to section 13 of the Payment Services Act for more information on exempt payment service providers.

Singapore.impact:
According to Singapore's de minimis rule, goods with a value of up to 400 SGD (approximately 208 SDR and 298 USD) are exempted from taxes and duties collected by customs. The deminimis threshold is applied to goods services tax on goods imported by post or air, excluding intoxicating liquors and tobacco. 

GST relief is granted on goods imported by post or air, excluding intoxicating liquors and tobacco, with a total Cost, Insurance and Freight (CIF) value not exceeding 400 SGD.
Since January 2023, GST has been extended to goods imported via air or post that are valued up to and including the current GST import relief threshold of S$400, through the Overseas Vendor Registration regime. The change ensures a level playing field for local businesses to compete effectively. The change also ensures that our GST system remains fair and resilient as the digital economy grows.

Singapore.impact: There is no custom duties on electronic transmission.

For a top-level ".sg" domain, a foreign registrant may register a domain name if it appoints a local agent having a valid Singaporean postal address as the Administrative Contact. 

For a "com.sg" domain, the company must be registered in Singapore. A foreign registrant may apply for a .com.sg domain name provided that it appoints a locally registered entity as its Administrative Contact.

The rules are pursuant to the Trade Marks Act (chapter 332)

Under section 368, every foreign company must establish a place of business in Singapore before carry on business in the country. A foreign company shall have a registered office in Singapore to which all communications and notices may be addressed and which shall be open and accessible to the public for not less than 5 hours between 9 a.m. and  5 p.m. each business day (Section 370). 

However, a registered office is not considered as local presence.

Singapore does not have specific consumer protection laws for e-commerce. However, a number of consumer laws which are in place (including the Consumer Protection (Fair Trading) Act 2003), which are generally applicable to all transactions including electronic transactions.

Singapore.impact: Singapore signed the United Nations Convention on the Use of Electronic Communications in International Contracts in 2006 (signed in 2006 and entered into force in 2013). Upon ratification, Singapore declared: The Convention shall not apply to electronic communications relating to any contract for the sale or other disposition of immovable property, or any interest in such property. The Convention shall also not apply in respect of (i) the creation or execution of a will; or (ii) the creation, performance or enforcement of an indenture, declaration of trust or power of attorney, that may be contracted for in any contract governed by the Convention.
